Grades
How will you get a grade?
Mostly via HW and Midterm / Final
6 or so assignments, you need to do 6 but it is easy to get full extra credit
If you do more than 6, you get extra credit (each HW is worth 10 points) but max score before exams is 70
Each assignment is worth the same
HW: 60 Midterm: 20 Final: 20
You need to take the Midterm and the Final regardless
Midterm and Final might be above 20 points; in which case these problems will be extra credit
HW is due about 2 weeks after it is assigned
The course objective: know enough NLP / ML from this class so you can impress on a job interview
If you want: you can write a survey paper and implement a model and/or try to extend some result and submit it: EMNLP / ACL / NAACL
I can help with this
5 pages is enough for a 'short' paper (can also be for a workshop)
Grades: To get A+, you need to do well on exams and HW
Score = min(sum(HW + Paper), 70) + Midterm + Final
Total points example (lots of extra HW but poor on exams): Score = (min(100, 70) + 5 + 5) = 80 = B-
Total points example (minimal HW but good on exams):
If you get a total Score ∼ 90 you get A-
If you get a total Score ∼ 100 you get A+ (you did well on exams and did the minimal HW)
If you get a total Score ∼ 93 you get A
If you get a total Score ∼ 82 you get B-
If you get a Score ∼ 78 you get C+
If you get a Score ∼ 62 you get D-
If you get a Score < 60 you get F
Might rescale this is needed to get some bell curve depending on the distribution; but I'll only rescale if it can help people

PyTorch Notebook Example
Logistic Regression Data
Consider Training data T where { ( x ( i ) , y ( i ) ) } N i =1 are observed and x ( i ) is some feature vector while y ( i ) is some response which is always 0/1 valued
Below, let ( x , y ) is a generic instance and each observation in { ( x ( i ) , y ( i ) ) } N i =1 is independent of any other
Let x 0 = 1 always; this is the 'intercept'
Other x j for j > 0 can be numerical or a part of a one-hot encodings for categorical features
For example,
or
We have 2 numerical features and 2 categorical features; one categorical has 3 levels and another has 2 levels
Recall one-hot encoding of categorical features: if f has k levels map each instance of f into a R k vector equal to 1 at that level and 0 otherwise (we'll see an example later)
Because y is 1/0, we aim to model y as a Bernoulli Random variable
Logistic Regression Assumptions
We assume that the probability y depends on x via a linear transformation
So y ∼ θ ⊺ x but we need a probability for y , since y is 1/0 and this probability sufficiently explains y
To get a probability, we use p ( y = 1 | x ; θ ) = g ( θ ⊺ x ) where g is some function that maps R → [0 , 1]
So we have:
θ ⊺ x can be anything, and so the domain of g is R
The range of g must be [0, 1] since g ( θ ⊺ x ) is a probability
This leads to a few variants:
Logistic Regression: g ( z ) = σ ( z ) = 1 1+ e -z
Probit Regression: g ( z ) = ∫ z -∞ e -t 2 / 2 √ 2 π dt
Logistic Regression with σ
σ ( z ) = 1 1+ e -z has some nice properties, for example
Note also that σ is monotonic in z
This implies, for example that we have p ( y = 1 | x ; θ ) = σ ( θ ⊺ x ) increasing in each θ j if all others are fixed
Note that this model is easy to interpret: if θ j > 0 this means that as x j gets larger p ( y = 1 | x ; θ ) gets larger
One-Hot representation: review this more if you need it
Features f that are categorical can be expressed as one-hot vectors: if f takes on 4 values f is a vector of 1/0 values and exactly one of these values is 1
Similarly, the response for logistic regression can be written as a one-hot vector: 0 can map to (1, 0) and 1 can map to (0, 1)
One-hot representations are important since they allow us to represent a categorical feature or response as a succinct vector
But they have a problem
Assume we have a feature f that has 4 categories and categories are very much related (say'Math' = 0, 'Physics' = 1, 'Painting' = 2, 'Writing' = 3)
If we have two instances f (1) = 'Math' and f (2) = 'Physics' which are have one-hot representations (1, 0, 0, 0) and (0, 1, 0, 0) then ( f (1) ) ⊺ f (2) = 0
But, this might not make sense: ideally the inner product captures some 'similarity' between the two vectors since Math and Physics are related

Linear Regression Assumptions
Here, we want to predict y from x but the y is continuous and real valued
In this case, we assume that y = θ ⊺ x + e where e is a normal mean 0 variance σ 2 random variable
The parameters are ( θ, σ 2 )
The above implies y is a normal random variable with mean θ ⊺ x and variance σ 2
This means p ( y | x ; θ, σ 2 ) = e -( y -θ ⊺ x ) 2 2 σ 2 √ 2 πσ 2 so that
\log p ( y | x ; \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } ) = - ( y - \theta ^ { \intercal } x ) ^ { 2 } / 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } - \log \sqrt { 2 \pi \sigma ^ { 2 } }
We again assume that given training data { ( x ( i ) , y ( i ) ) } N i =1 each observation pair is independent of any other From all this, you can derive the likelihood of the data

\mathcal { L } ( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } ) = p ( y ^ { ( 1 ) } , \dots , y ^ { ( N ) } | x ^ { ( 1 ) } , \dots , x ^ { ( N ) } ; \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } ) = \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } p ( y ^ { ( i ) } | x ^ { ( i ) } ; \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } )
and the goal is max θ,σ 2 L ( θ, σ 2 )
Linear Regression: Log-Likelihood
The above simplifies a bit to give

\mathcal { L } ( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } ) = \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \frac { e ^ { - ( y ^ { ( i ) } - \theta ^ { \tau } x ^ { ( i ) } ) ^ { 2 } / 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } } } { \sqrt { 2 \pi \sigma ^ { 2 } } }
and the goal is max θ,σ 2 L ( θ, σ 2 )
From this, you can derive the log-likelihood of the data

\ell ( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } ) = \log \mathcal { L } ( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } ) = [ \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } - ( y ^ { ( i ) } - \theta ^ { \top } x ^ { ( i ) } ) ^ { 2 } / 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } ] - \frac { N } { 2 } \log 2 \pi \sigma ^ { 2 }
and the goal is max θ,σ 2 ℓ ( θ, σ 2 )
Since log is increasing, notice that
\max _ { \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } } \mathcal { L } ( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } ) \sim \max _ { \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } } \ell ( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } )
and the max is attained at the same point
Linear Regression: Loss
We have

\ell ( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } ) = \log \mathcal { L } ( \theta , \sigma ^ { 2 } ) = [ \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } - ( y ^ { ( i ) } - \theta ^ { T } x ^ { ( i ) } ) ^ { 2 } / 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } ] - \frac { N } { 2 } \log 2 \pi \sigma ^ { 2 }
and the goal is max θ,σ 2 ℓ ( θ, σ 2 )
If you focus on just the the part dependent on θ , it is clear that we want to minimize
\mathcal { J } ( \theta ) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } ( y ^ { ( i ) } - \theta ^ { \top } x ^ { ( i ) } ) ^ { 2 }
so that the goal is min θ J ( θ )
Typically, since J ( θ ) is minimized, we call this a loss and min θ J ( θ ) is equivalent to max θ ℓ ( θ, σ 2 )
The optimization for σ 2 is easy, no need to make a loss vs log-likelihood connection

Optimization for Logistic Regression
Back to Logistic Regression
How do we optimize for θ ?
Typically, we have data { ( x ( i ) , y ( i ) ) } N i =1 and we write down the likelihood function, then we optimize
Assume we have
p ( y & = 1 | x ; \theta ) = \sigma ( \theta ^ { \top } x ) \\ p ( y & = 0 | x ; \theta ) = 1 - \sigma ( \theta ^ { \top } x )
Note that this can be written very easily as
p ( y | x ; \theta ) = \sigma ( \theta ^ { \top } x ) ^ { y } ( 1 - \sigma ( \theta ^ { \top } \succ ) ) ^ { 1 - y }
Optimization for Logistic Regression
One assumption of Logistic Regression is that for each i training samples are independent
This means that if we write the likelihood of the data we have
This simplifies more to

\mathcal { L } ( \theta ) = \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } p ( y ^ { ( i ) } | x ^ { ( i ) } ; \theta ) = \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \sigma ( \theta ^ { \top } x ^ { ( i ) } ) ^ { y ^ { ( i ) } } ( 1 - \sigma ( \theta ^ { \top } x ^ { ( i ) } ) ) ^ { 1 - y ^ { ( i ) } }
As with Linear Regression, we want to find
\max _ { \theta } \mathcal { L } ( \theta )
How do we optimize ℓ ( θ )
As before, we take the logarithm and set the log-likelihood to ℓ ( θ ) = log L ( θ ) and we have
\max _ { \theta } \mathcal { L } ( \theta ) \sim \max _ { \theta } \ell ( \theta )
We get
\ell ( \theta ) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } [ y ^ { ( i ) } \log \sigma ( \theta ^ { \top } x ^ { ( i ) } ) + ( 1 - y ^ { ( i ) } ) \log 1 - \sigma ( \theta ^ { \top } x ^ { ( i ) } ) ]
Some Questions:
How do you optimize this function?
What do the gradients look like?
What's the general idea and can we eventually build on this idea for more complicated (Neural) models?
Binary Cross-Entropy Step by Step
For y 1/0 and p some probability which tries to match y ∼ p
Then, the Cross-Entropy between y and p is
- y \log p - ( 1 - y ) \log ( 1 - p )
If p is close to y , this Cross-Entropy is 0 and the number above represents how far p is from the truth y
Consider one term -y log a -(1 -y ) log(1 -a ) for some ( x , y ) so p = a = σ ( θ ⊺ x )
Suppose we have some x and have a true label y = 1 without loss of generality
Then, the above becomes -log a
This is the term controlled by the model: the model, based on its parameters θ , sets a since a = σ ( z ) = σ ( θ ⊺ x )
A good model is such that in the above case a is close to 1, since we want to minimize and 0 ≤ a ≤ 1
Similarly, you can argue that if y = 0 then a good model has a ∼ 0 since we need to minimize -log 1 -a
Binary Cross-Entropy For Entire Dataset
Take z ( i ) = θ ⊺ x ( i )
Then the Cross-Entropy J ( θ ) is the negative of the log-likelihood ℓ ( θ ) across the data and
\mathcal { J } ( \theta ) = - \ell ( \theta ) = - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \left [ y ^ { ( i ) } \log a ^ { ( i ) } + ( 1 - y ^ { ( i ) } ) \log 1 - a ^ { ( i ) } \right ]
Essentially, for each i we are modeling p ( y ( i ) = 1 | x ( i ) ; θ ) and we want to push θ as needed so a ( i ) = σ ( θ ⊺ x ( i ) ) is close to y ( i ) on average across all i
So, the goal of Logistic Regression is to find a θ that is good on average across all points { ( x ( i ) , y ( i ) ) } N i =1
When y ( i ) is 1 we want σ ( θ ⊺ x ( i ) ) to also be 1 and similarly for y ( i ) = 0
Gradient Descent / Ascent and Optimizing J ( θ )
To do an optimization of the form min θ J ( θ ) we can use Full (Batch) Gradient Descent
The algorithm goes as follows:
Pick a learning rate ρ > 0
Pick an initial value θ 0
For t = 1 , . . . , T
Set θ t = θ t -1 -ρ ∇J ( θ t -1 )
Return θ T
There are other variants of this, but at a high level this is the idea
Recall we have
- \max _ { \theta } \ell ( \theta ) \sim \min _ { \theta } \mathcal { J } ( \theta )
For the log-likelihood maximization the update rule looks like θ t = θ t -1 + ρ ∇ ℓ ( θ t -1 ) where we want to solve max θ ℓ ( θ )
Mini Batch Gradient Descent
To do an optimization of the form min θ J ( θ ) we usually use Mini Batch Gradient Descent
This is needed to speed things up - we update θ much more often!
Big idea: break up the Training data T into batched T 1 , . . . , T K which are disjoint and cover T
At a high level, the algorithm goes as follows:
Pick a learning rate ρ > 0
Pick an initial value θ = θ 0
For t = 1 , . . . , T
For k = 1 , . . . , K
Get the loss J T k ( θ ) over just this batch T k
Denote by ∇J T k ( θ ) the gradient of this loss just over this batch
Set θ = θ -ρ ∇J T k ( θ )
Return θ
Gradients for Logistic Regression
The core idea lies in finding gradients: J T k ( θ ) and similar terms
How do we get the gradients with data? Do we need them analytically always?
No! In PyTorch / Tensorflow there are numerical methods that give us the gradients automatically; we'll see some examples as we go
But it is good to know what the gradients are so we understand what is going on
What do the gradients look like for Logistic Regression?
Consider just one training example ( x , y )
Logistic Regression Gradients: Let's compute them in a general way!
Let's consider the maximization max θ ℓ ( θ )
What is ∂ℓ ∂θ j ( θ ) ?
Let's try to do this with something called a computation graph, since this is what we'll need for Neural Networks
Assume x has dimension 1 for the sake of argument, so that θ ⊺ x = β x + α and we have just two parameters β ∈ R , α ∈ R
How do we write out the dependencies between things as a graph?
We have 3 'leaves', x , α , β
We have z = β x + α
We have a = σ ( z )
We have ℓ ( α, β ) = y log a +(1 -y ) log(1 -a ) and we want ∂ℓ ∂α and ∂ℓ ∂β so we can do gradient optimization

Logistic Regression Computational Graph
Note: this is a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)
The parameters and data are the leaves: they have no 'in' arcs
There are no cycles
Generally, this will be how graphs look like and these graphs help us reason about what the chain rule gives so we can get gradients
How do we find ∂ℓ ∂α ?
The chain rule can be written as starting from ℓ and considering all paths to α
There is just 1 path in this case
α → z → a → ℓ
This mean that
\frac { \partial \ell } { \partial \alpha } = ( \frac { \partial \ell } { \partial a } ) ( \frac { \partial a } { \partial z } ) ( \frac { \partial z } { \partial \alpha } )
Note that you must remember the chain rule ... In the above
On the left, we have gradients
On the right, we have local gradients
Logistic Regression Computational Graph
We have
\frac { \partial \ell } { \partial \alpha } = ( \frac { \partial \ell } { \partial a } ) ( \frac { \partial a } { \partial z } ) ( \frac { \partial z } { \partial \alpha } )
We have ℓ = y log a +(1 -y ) log(1 -a )
This means ∂ℓ ∂ a = y a -1 -y 1 -a = y -a a (1 -a )
We have a = σ ( z )
This means ∂ a ∂ z = σ ( z )(1 -σ ( z )) = a (1 -a )
We have z = β x + α
This means ∂ z ∂α = 1
This means
\frac { \partial \ell } { \partial \alpha } = ( \frac { y - a } { a ( 1 - a ) } ) ( a ( 1 - a ) ) = y - a
Similarly, you can show that
\frac { \partial \ell } { \partial \beta } = ( \frac { y - a } { a ( 1 - a ) } ) ( a ( 1 - a ) ) x = ( y - a ) x
Logistic Regression Computational Graph
There is an recipe in all this
To do Gradient Descent / Ascent you need gradients and gradients can be gotten by considering a graph like before:
Fix your current parameter θ old = ( α old , β old )
Draw DAG: write down the graph above to understand the relationships between steps
Forward Pass: compute z , a , ℓ as above numerically by plugging in x , α old , β old and going forward
Backward Pass: use the graph above (and the values) to compute ∂ℓ ∂α ( α old , β old ) and ∂ℓ ∂β ( α old , β old )
Update: Use the above to update parameters; we have
\alpha _ { n e w } = \alpha _ { o l d } + \rho \frac { \partial \ell } { \partial \alpha } ( \alpha _ { o l d } )
\beta _ { n e w } = \beta _ { o l d } + \rho \frac { \partial \ell } { \partial \beta } ( \beta _ { o l d } )
and
Repeat ...
For example, we could have done batch gradient ascent with batches of size 1; the above is exactly what we would do!
Another Example: Overfitting and the DAG for this case
When we fit a model to the training data, we usually don't just care about Training performance
We also care about performance on another separate Test set
Typically, to control this, we add a regularization term R ( θ ) ≥ 0 which is positive and which controls model complexity
The 2-norm is R ( θ ) = ∑ d j =1 θ 2 j and typically this makes θ j small but = 0
The 1-norm is R ( θ ) = ∑ d j =1 | θ j | and typically this makes θ sparse so that very few θ j are nonzero
Given the regularization, we fix λ > 0 and then focus on max θ ℓ ( θ ) -λ R ( θ )
Note that, the larger the λ the more we focus on R ( θ )
If λ →∞ then we get θ = 0 since R ( θ ) dominates
The right λ usually needs to be tuned in some way; it is a hyperparameter
Computational Graph For Logistic Regression with Regularization
Assume we pick R ( θ ) = ∑ d j =1 θ 2 j so that the optimization we consider is
\max _ { \theta } \ell ( \theta ) - \lambda ( \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { d } \theta _ { j } ^ { 2 } ) = \max _ { \theta } \ell ^ { \lambda } ( \theta )
Assume θ = ( α, β ) ∈ R 2 as before so that R ( θ ) = α 2 + β 2
What does the computational graph look like?
What are the gradients?
We'll do this very carefully, so that the last node is ℓ λ

Computing the Gradients again
What is ∂ℓ λ ∂α ?
In this case, we have two paths
α → z → a → ℓ → ℓ λ
α →R→ ℓ λ
We have

\frac { \partial \ell ^ { \lambda } } { \partial \alpha } = ( \frac { \partial \ell ^ { \lambda } } { \partial \ell } ) ( \frac { \partial \ell } { \partial a } ) ( \frac { \partial a } { \partial z } ) ( \frac { \partial z } { \partial \alpha } ) + ( \frac { \partial \ell ^ { \lambda } } { \partial \mathcal { R } } ) ( \frac { \partial \mathcal { R } } { \partial \alpha } )
Computing the Gradients again
We have
\ell ^ { \lambda } = \ell - \lambda \mathcal { R } = y \log a + ( 1 - y ) \log ( 1 - a ) - \lambda ( \alpha ^ { 2 } + \beta ^ { 2 } )
This means ∂ℓ λ ∂ a = ( ∂ℓ λ ∂ℓ )( ∂ℓ ∂ a ) = (1)( y a -1 -y 1 -a ) = y -a a (1 -a )
On the other hand we have the local ∂ℓ λ ∂ R = -λ and ∂ R ∂α = 2 α
We have a = σ ( z )
\ > \ T h i s \text { means } \frac { \partial a } { \partial z } = \sigma ( z ) ( 1 - \sigma ( z ) ) = a ( 1 - a )
We have z = β x + α
This means ∂ z ∂α = 1
This means
\frac { \partial \ell ^ { \lambda } } { \partial \alpha } = ( \frac { y - a } { a ( 1 - a ) } ) ( a ( 1 - a ) ) - 2 \lambda \alpha = y - a - 2 \lambda \alpha
Similarly, you can derive ∂ℓ λ ∂β
Computing the Gradients again
This Computational Graph idea is very useful
At a high level, this is just the chain rule but the graph helps us reason about the terms we need
We'll see many more examples in the class but it's crucial that you understand this example

AUC: A Review
What makes a model good at classification for 1/0 response?
Logistic Regression gives us two types of probabilities, p ( y = 1) and p ( y = 0)
Imagine you have a imbalanced data, where 95% of the data is y = 0 and y = 1 is only 5% of the data
Accuracy is not good here, since trivial classifiers give good results
A random classifier (predicting y = 1 with 5%) would give an accuracy of approximately ∼ 90%
A deterministic classifier that always predicts 0 would give an accuracy of 95% too
Other metrics, like Recall and Precision came about to address this; an intuitive one you should know is AUC
There are a few ways to understand AUC (i.e. with ROC graphics) but this one you might not know
What should a good model do?
Generally, we would hope it gives a higher score p ( y = 1) on average to response cases with y = 1 (positives) than with y = 0 (negatives)
This is the idea of the AUC; let P and N be the positive and negative samples we have in a dataset and g be our model, which returns p ( y = 1)
A good model, intuitively, gives class 1 scores close to 1 for y ∈ P and close to zero for y ∈ N
For a Logistic Regression, this is g ( x ) = σ ( θ ⊺ x ) and notice that this is p ( y = 1)
Let 1 A be the indicator of A : 1 if A is true and 0 otherwise
We then have
A U C = \frac { \sum _ { i \in \mathcal { P } } \frac { \sum _ { j \in \mathcal { N } } 1 _ { \{ g ( x ^ { ( i ) } ) > g ( x ^ { ( j ) } ) \} } } { | \mathcal { N } | } } { | \mathcal { P } | }
AUC Intuition
A U C = \frac { \sum _ { i \in \mathcal { P } } \frac { \sum _ { j \in \mathcal { N } } 1 _ { \{ g ( x ^ { ( i ) } ) > g ( x ^ { ( j ) } ) \} } } { | \mathcal { N } | } } { | \mathcal { P } | }
Put another way:
A good model should score points in P higher than it does points in N
The AUC as expressed above is an average, where we consider the average number of times a particular positive point is higher than all negative ones
Then, we average over all positive points
What is a bad AUC?
If you consider the above, you can reason that a 50% value is bad, as it would be the AUC of a random classifier
Why don't we just optimize AUC?
If we care about AUC, why don't we just optimize it directly?
A U C = \frac { \sum _ { i \in \mathcal { P } } \frac { \sum _ { j \in \mathcal { N } } 1 _ { \{ g ( x ^ { ( i ) } ) > g ( x ^ { ( j ) } ) \} } } { | \mathcal { N } | } } { | \mathcal { P } | }
The above does not have a gradient that's easy to compute
Loosely, a higher Log-Likelihood leads to a higher AUC
Crucially, the Log-Likelihood is much easier to optimize
AUC in PyTorch and leading into Examples
from torch import tensor from torchmetrics import AUROC
target = tensor ([0, 0, 1, 1, 11) auroc = AUROC (task="binary") auroc (preds, target) [> tensor (0.5000 )
For now, think of a tensor as a type of NumPy array
Here, |P| = 3 and |N| = 2
Try to compute this manually to make sure you understand it
What if we reverse y = 1 and y = 0
Notice that AUC is computed with respect to the class y = 1
A U C = \frac { \sum _ { i \in \mathcal { P } } \frac { \sum _ { j \in \mathcal { N } } 1 _ { \{ g ( x ^ { ( i ) } ) > g ( x ^ { ( j ) } ) \} } } { | \mathcal { N } | } } { | \mathcal { P } | }
For class y = 0, you can do the same but the equation becomes as below since 1 -g ( x ) is the probability that y = 0
A U C = \frac { \sum _ { j \in \mathcal { N } } \frac { \sum _ { i \in \mathcal { P } } 1 _ { \{ 1 - g ( x ^ { ( j ) } ) > 1 - g ( x ^ { ( i ) } ) \} } { | \mathcal { P } | } } { | \mathcal { N } | }
This is since for a true class y = 0 we expect the 0 score for these 1 -g ( x ) to be larger than the zero scores for a true class y = 1
But this is the same thing
The above formula is
A U C = \frac { \sum _ { j \in \mathcal { N } } \frac { \sum _ { i \in \mathcal { P } } 1 _ { \{ 1 - g ( x ^ { ( j ) } ) > 1 - g ( x ^ { ( i ) } ) \} } { | \mathcal { P } | } } { | \mathcal { N } | }
This is the same as (rearranging inequalities)
A U C = \frac { \sum _ { j \in \mathcal { N } } \frac { \sum _ { i \in \mathcal { P } } 1 _ { \{ g ( x ^ { ( i ) } ) > g ( x ^ { ( j ) } ) \} } } { | \mathcal { P } | } } { | \mathcal { N } | }
Which is the same as (swamp sums)
A U C = \frac { \sum _ { i \in \mathcal { P } } \frac { \sum _ { j \in \mathcal { N } } 1 _ { \{ g ( x ^ { ( i ) } ) > g ( x ^ { ( j ) } ) \} } } { | \mathcal { N } | } } { | \mathcal { P } | }
These are the same thing!
What about Multiclass AUC?
Generally, for a model predicting K classes, we get a vector of probabilities p ( i ) = ( p ( i ) 1 , p ( i ) 2 , ..., p ( i ) K ) for each x ( i )
Here, K is the number of classes
How do we get an AUC in this case?
Fix a class k and for each point i take just p ( i ) k
Label each y ( i ) as 1/0 where 1 means the true class is k and 0 is anything else
Compute a AUC k which is like the AUC in 2 dimensions for probability p ( i ) k and the vector y ( i )
Do this for all k
Average over all the k = 1 , . . . , K
This is your Multiclass AUC,
A U C = \frac { \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { K } A U C _ { k } } { K }
How do you use AUC?
Say you have 3 positive samples and 987 negative samples
You see 97 % AUC; how do you interpret this?
On average, across the 3 samples, this means that the score of one of the positives is larger than 97 % of the score given to true negatives
So, this means that on average, for each positive samples, we expect to have 0.97 * 987 = 957 negatives we score higher than
So, this means that there are roughly 30 negatives for each positive that have a higher probability score given by the model; the model is breaking down for these

On the other hand, random sampling has another problem: the 'unreliable tail' (see previous slide)
There are several tokens with small probabilities that should not be sampled, but are given a chance and result in gibberish
To compare: Pick generated text and compare the probability of the generated words to some reference, such as human text
Top K Sampling
One direct way to remove the tail is TopK sampling
This is easy to explain: at each time step, consider only the K tokens with the highest probabilities
When decoding at time t , select a set V t K of K top tokens
Set C = ∑ x ∈V t K p ( x | x 0 , . . . , x t -1 )
Renormalize and sample using q ( x | x 0 , . . . , x t -1 ) = p ( x | x 0 , . . . , x t -1 ) / C
Good: This method removes tokens with a low probability, so this is good because it truncates the tail
Problem: What is K ? Should K always be the same?
Problem: To match human text, we need K large; this leads to incoherence
Problem: I.e. You need low probability words sometime
Problem: q above varies greatly at each time step, hard to understand the gears

Temperature Scaling: Fixing the unreliable tail
Fix T > 0
Consider the token distribution p = softmax ( a ) but this time work with q = softmax ( a / T )
Let T → 0
As we saw, this has the effect of pushing tokens with high probabilities higher, and suppressing tokens with lower probabilities
Low temperature sampling can be used to partially alleviate the flatness / tail mass issues of TopK sampling discussed above; by shaping the distribution before TopK sampling
Problem: While lowering the temperature improves generation quality and eliminates the tail, it comes at the cost of decreasing diversity (see paper)

Self-BLEU and Diversity
Given a set of hypotheses { G 1 , G 2 , . . . , G T } , how do we measure diversity?
For each generation, consider the BLEU score when we look at G t vs the rest, with the rest serving as references
We have:
If the Self-BLEU is small, we have a large diversity (Eg. large K in TopK , random sampling)
If the Self-BLEU is large, we have a low diversity (Eg. Beam Search, Low temperature, small K in TopK )

Goal: From scratch, train a smaller BERT and have it get similar performance
Specifically, get the same (or very close) performance to BERT but with fewer parameters
3 Main Optimizations
Do not use the same embedding dimension for input tokens as the hidden dimensions
Replace NSP with Sentence Order Prediction (SOP)
Share parameters between Encoder transformer blocks
Using less parameters for token Embeddings
For BERT, if E is the token (vocab, position, or type) embedding dimension and H is the hidden dimension, E = H
If the vocabulary is size E , this means O ( V × E ) = O ( V × H ) parameters for the main (noncontextual) token embeddings
Instead, make E << H
Project E to dimension H , then do the same
The above has O ( V × E ) + O ( E × H ) parameters
Idea: Pick E so that the space needed for token parameters
O ( V \times E ) + O ( E \times H ) < < O ( V \times H )

Big Idea: It's not just all about more parameters!
Vision Transformer (ViT)
After BERT came out, other variants like ALBERT came out
Some dealt with text, but others dealt with other types of data as well
We saw CNNs for image classification and we used them to even do text classification
Can we take the Transformer architectures we've seen for text and use them for image classification (i.e. the reverse)?
Answer: Yes
Vision Transformer
At a high level, this is how a Vision Transformer works ...
Take the Transformer Encoder and use it to somehow ingest a picture and predict an image class
But for a Transform we had tokens, what are the tokens here?
Take a 224 × 224 × 3 picture
Break it up into patches of size 16 × 16 × 3
Each of these patches are 'tokens' and we need them to be vectors so we map (using a CNN or MLP) each small volume to a vector
For a Transformer, we need to break ties using positional embeddings, so add these
As usual, feed everything through the Transformer blocks and get the final representations
But, we need a prediction to make gradient updates and learn ... Add a CLS token at the start of the token sequence
Map the final CLS token's embedding through a MLP and predict the class of the picture (MNIST, CIFAR-10, etc)
Back propagate!
Given enough data, we get a very powerful classifier

Fine Tuning
Given a transformer, what options do we have to use it for a specific task?
Let's focus on Classification and BERT, where we add a MLP on top of the CLS token
Option1: We can optimize just the 'head' MLP parameters
Using BERT as a feature extractor
This is relatively cheap
We do not, however, get SOTA on tasks
Option 2: We can optimize ALL of the parameters (BERT + MLP)
This is fine-tuning
We can get SOTA
But, the big problem is that this is quite expensive
Is there something in-between?
We saw one example with ULM-Fit: different learning rates were used per layer
Use adapter
Adapter
"model

The idea of an adapter is to insert a few critical layers in a transformer and allow gradients only on parameters from these layers The adapter also contains a skip-connection. During adapter tuning, the green layers are trained on the downstream data, this includes the adapter, the layer normalization parameters, and the
If you pick the architecture right, you can save time on fine tuning
Generally, this will increase the inference cost
Big idea: insert a small MLP, tune the LayerNorm parameters
Let d model be the embedding dimensions, and d adapter be the MLP
Total gradient parameters per transformer block:
MLP: 2 d model d adapter + d model + d adapter
LayerNorm: 4 d model
Use d adapter << d model
Adapter vs Fine Tuning
Adapter-based tuning requires training two orders of magnitude fewer parameters to fine-tuning, while attaining similar performance -10-15

Adapter Robustness
Ablating one layer's Adaptor makes no difference
Adapters on the lower layers have a smaller impact than the higher-layers - those are the crucial ones (read across rows and see last column)
Row i , Column j : Don't adapt layers i to j
One intuition is that the lower layers extract lower-level features that are shared among tasks, while the higher layers build features that are unique to different task
This indicates that although each adapter has a small influence on the overall network, the overall effect is large
The performance of adapters is robust to initialization
However, when the initialization is too large, performance degrades for COLA

LORA
Both adapters and other methods like prefix-tuning (see references) have some issues
Adapters introduce high inference latency
Prefix-tuning is difficult to optimize, and performance changes non-monotonically in trainable parameters
Also, reserving a part of the sequence length for adaptation necessarily reduces the sequence length available to process a downstream task; might this lead to degradation in performance?
LORA

LORA: For each matrix W in a transformer, for example a key matrix W k , set W k = W k + BA where B , A have low rank r << min { d k , d model } and B ∈ R dk × r , A ∈ R r × dmodel Pretrained Weights WE Raxa
LORA: Low-Rank Adaptation
Initialize B to all zeros and A to Gaussian noise so BA = 0 at the start but gradient updates are not trivially 'stuck'
Benefits of LORA
Why would LORA be a good idea?
Generalized full fine-tuning - if we select r to to the right choice and have a LORA set B , A for each matrix, we basically have fine tuning
Adapters converge to an MLP
Prefix models cannot handle longer sequences
Benefit of LORA: no additional layers, so no additional inference latency
Another benefit: at test time, we can use W = W + BA , just a new matrix ... We always need to load 1 model
Yet another: we can swap in different BA for different tasks, so it's easy to 'adapt' the model
For their paper, the authors focus on just the Self Attention parameters like W k or W q ; they freeze all the other parameters

Is the 'optimal' adaptation matrix BA really rank- deficient? If so, what is a good rank to use in practice?
Even a very small rank does well: it is more of a choice of which matrices to apply the method to
What is the connection between BA and W ? Does BA highly correlate with W ? How large is BA compared to W ?
Core idea: use the Frobenius norm of a matrix || M || = √ ∑ i σ 2 i
SVD: For any M, we have M = U Σ V
We can use U , V to project a matrix into another matrix's space
We can use U , V to understand 'correlation' between matrices
LORA: BA = ∆ W vs W
LORA: BA = ∆ W vs W
First, BA has a stronger correlation with W compared to a random matrix, indicating that BA amplifies some features that are already in W 0.32 0.02
Second, instead of repeating the top singular directions of W , BA only amplifies directions that are not emphasized in W
Third, the amplification factor is rather huge: 21.5 ∼ 6.91/0.32 for r = 4 (Here, they project W q into the space of ∇ W q and then compare the Frobenius norm W q with this projection)
Hypothesis: LORA matrix potentially amplifies the important features for specific downstream tasks that were learned but not emphasized in the general pre-training model

Recommendation Problems: What do we need to do?
Given a problem, there are many things to consider
What data do we have?
What labels do we have?
What models do we use?
How do we know what metrics to look at? Offline metrics? Online metrics?
What is the scale? Is it serving fast enough?
Do recommendations need to be fresh upon request or can they be somewhat stale?

Metrics
Let's step back a second ...
Suppose we have a model such that, given some query, gives us the most relevant items ranked from best to worst
How do we know that this is doing well?
Goal: Have a metric such that if we do well we have a value of 1 if we do poorly we have a value of 0
'Well' here means that the results returned are relevant to the user
Offline Metrics: Recall
For a certain set of queries, we might know the items that should be returned
Suppose our system always returns 5 items ranked from best to worst
Suppose that for a query q we have a universe of items I ( q ) which are relevant
For some query, this might be 1 item while for another query this might be 1000 items
Given we made 5 ranked predictions, we can then compute
R e c a l l \ 5 = \frac { \text {Number of relevant items among the top 5 predictions} } { | I ( q ) | }
Offline Metrics: Recall
What's the idea: we've made 5 predictions, and we want to know of these 5 how many actually are sensible with respect to the universe of all relevant items
But there might be a problem ... What is the Recall of the below 2 predictions?
Offline Metrics: Recall
XX
In the above, we have 2 systems that make 5 predictions
Each system has 2 relevant items, 3 irrelevant items
Given the definitions, we have
System 1 has a Recall of 2 | I ( q ) |
System 2 has a Recall of 2 | I ( q ) |
Both the same! But notice that
System 1 has a relevant item in position 1
System 2 has no relevant items in any of the top 3 positions
Moreover, what if | I ( q ) | ∼ 1000 i.e. very large? In this case, the best Recall metric would be 2 1000 , very small!
We should be able to differentiate better!
Offline Metrics: Recall
So we have issues with Recall ...
Recall does not take into account the position of relevant and irrelevant items in the predictions - it just uses the hits count
If there are many irrelevant items, Recall might be hard to work with ... The maximum Recall for the above two examples is 5 | I ( q ) | and this maximum can be very small
What else can we do? For the above, we can vary k and look at Recall @ k
Offline Metrics: Recall @ k
We have the definition
R e c a l l \oplus k = \frac { \text {Number of relevant items among the top $k$ predictions} } { | I ( q ) | }
Why might we do this?
Well, we want to know the System 1 > System 2 and we would ascertain this if we look at the Recall @1 and Recall @ 2 and Recall @ 5, for example
System 1: 1 | I ( q ) | , 0, 2 | I ( q ) |
System 2: 0, 0, 2 | I ( q ) |

Here, we are looking at the Recall using just the top k predictions ... Recall @ 1 being higher means System 1 puts relevant items at the front whereas System 2 only puts relevant items in positions 4 and 5
Offline Metrics: Precision @ k

We saw with Recall that the denominator can become small ... We are making k predictions so ideally we have a metric which can be at most 1 and worst 0 ... Recall @ k can be at worst 0 and at most k | I ( q ) |
We have the definition
Here, we make k predictions at at most k of them are relevant ... So the fraction above is at most 1 and at worst 0 but we are not sure if we always can touch 1

Offline Metrics: Precision
ANXI
For the two systems above, notice that Precision @ 5 is 2/5 in both cases ... What else? How do we get a better understanding?
Like before, we need to vary @
System 1 Precision @ 1: 1 ... The fact that this is 1 makes it easy to understand
System 2 Precision @ 1: 0 ... The fact that this is 0 shows us how bad this system is vs System 1
Offline Metrics: Precision Issues
ANXI
▶ Any issues?
As before, we have Precision @ 5 is the same for both systems ... We don't take specific position relevance into account
What if the number of items that can be relevant is very small, for example 2? Then, at best, the Precision @ 5 is at most 2 5 ... So we have sort of the opposite problem we had with Recall
Offline Metrics: AP
x^
We can get a sense of the positional performance of a system by looking at Precision @ k where k is 1, 2, . . . ... What else?
Absolute Precision (AP) - Mixing Recall and Precision ... Suppose rel(i) = 1/0 if the i th prediction is relevant or not ... Then we have
AP \, \mathbb { O } \, k = \frac { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } P _ { \text {Precision} } \, \mathbb { O } \, i \, \mathbb { * } \, r e l ( i ) } { | I ( q ) | }
Notice that this metric is again between 0 and 1 so again we have a metric which is lowest at 0 and has the highest value 1
Offline Metrics: AP Example and Issues
ANXI
With this metric, we reward the system if it puts relevant items at the front, not just if there is a hit

Notice we could have also divided by 5 if | I ( q ) | was this value, but then the maximum value of this metric if there are at most 2 relevant items would be (1 + 1) / 5 = 2/10 ... So again, this is hard to interpret
Offline Metrics: AP Issues with alternative definition
To do Average Precision right you really should have k be dependent on I ( q ) since the range of AP depends on I ( q )
For example, one query might have 5 relevant items and another might have 2
... So, if you want to see how well you do on all queries you might want to consider k to be the maximum of I ( q ) for all queries
Specifically, with this k , for either of the above queries, the maximum AP is 1 and you really only get rewarded if you put things at the front for all queries
To make this clear, we have that for two queries q 1 and q 2 let's says I ( q 1 ) = 100 and I ( q 2 ) = 4
Let's say we have k = 5
Then, maximally, the AP for these 2 queries would be 5 / 100 and 2 / 4, respectively
Another definition: We can ignore I ( q ) and divided by the total number of relevant items in the top k predictions so we don't depend on I ( q ) ... Specifically, we can also define
Offline Metrics: AP without I ( q
AP \circledast k = \frac { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } P _ { \text {Precision} } \circledast i * \circledast ( i ) } { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \text {rel} ( i ) }

I.e. Suppose for and we have 2 systems which produce the results below ... Which is better?
System 1: AP = (1+1)/2 = 1
System 2: AP = (1)/1 = 1
In this example, both systems don't put relevant items (of the 5) in the front and System 1 puts more! They are not the same!
Offline Metrics: Mean Average Precision (mAP)
So what if we now have a fixed System and for the 2 different queries above it returns the two ranking orders above? Can we somehow aggregate the full health of the system?
Yes: get the AP for both queries and then take the mean
For the above example where we predict 5 items maximally, this is (3/4 + 13/40) / 2 since these are the AP of both ranking orders and we have two queries
Here, we assume I ( q ) = 2 is the same for both queries ...
Offline Metrics: MRR - mAP with just 1 relevant possibility
What if we have a set results for a given set of queries and for each query we can have at most 1 relevant item?
In this case, we can compute the Mean Reciprocal Rank, which measures how well are at putting the relevant item at the front
Imagine search, where perhaps there is just one item we can have at the front!
Offline Metrics: MRR
Another example is above ... Using the second AP definition, this has mAP of (1/4 + 1 + 1/3) / 3
Given m queries q we can name this special case Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR @ k ) as
M R R \notin k = \frac { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } 1 / r a n k _ { i } } { m }
rank i is the rank of the first relevant result for query i in the k presented results
Offline Metrics: MRR - Don't use it if you can have more than 1 relevant item
Given m queries q we can name this special case Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR @ k ) as
M R R \notin k & = \frac { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } 1 / r a n k _ { i } } { m } \\
In the above, for each query, the ranki term is the rank of the first relevant item in the list of k predictions
Notice that, if we have a more than 1 relevant item, this metric does not care about the ranking of that item
MRR is very good when there is 1 relevant item and mAP is in a sense a generalization of MRR, which works for more than 1 relevant item
If there is 1 relevant item at most per query, MRR = mAP
Note: to understand this, we might want k large to see how far down the

Offline Metrics: Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (nDCG) 1 4 2
Sometimes, given a list of items, we have not just yes/no marker on the item but a relevance score per item
In this case, it might make sense to give a reward to a metric not just using the position and relevant / irrelevant but using the actual score we assign
For example, suppose we rank 5 items as below and we assign a score to each item as below ... Suppose we have a rank score of 0-5 for each item
For example, the second item is very relevant but the first item is completely irrelevant
Given this, we define the Discounted Cumulative Gain at 5 as
Offline Metrics: nDCG
D C G \mathcal { O } k = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \frac { \ r e l _ { i } } { \log _ { 2 } \left ( i + 1 \right ) }
For the above, the value we get is 6.151
This metric uses our score to reward; there is some theoretical justification for the logarithms
For any query, the metric value can be all over the place however, so it is better for us to use a normalized version of this metric
Offline Metrics: nDCG
Suppose the IDEAL ranking scores for this query are as above
We can compute the DCG of this, and call it IDCG
For this example, we would get 11.4
Given this, we define the Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain as
n D C G = \frac { D C G } { I D C G }
For our example, we get nDCG = 6 . 151 / 11 . 4
Offline Metrics: AUC
As a final metric, recall we have AUC given by

A U C = \frac { \sum _ { i \in \mathcal { P } } \frac { \sum _ { j \in \mathcal { N } } 1 _ { \{ \mathcal { G } ( q , v _ { i } ) > \mathcal { G } ( q , v _ { j } ) \} } { | \mathcal { N } | } } { | \mathcal { P } | }
For a query, can compute this metric and notice we can use a score which associates a query to each video
For each query, there might be 5 relevant videos or 100 so P and N are naturally defined
I.e., we don't need to worry so much about k , this naturally will take care of itself
Of course, an average AUC of 100 % across all queries will tell us that our System is doing very well - all positives are ahead of all negatives
What k do we display? Maybe we have a dynamic one or an average one given | I ( q ) | on average
Online Metrics: CTR, CVR, CVS?
All the metrics we've discussed above are Offline Metrics: we have a machine learning model and we want to get it to rank things as well as possible
The analysis we do is done in a research / notebook setting, using historical data ... What happens once we deploy?
In that setting, a user types a query and then gets list of results
... How do we tell if the results were good? How do we build a larger system that uses the ranking model we evaluated?
Let's first look at the basic metrics which we would use to evaluate System-level performance
When we present the user items, they can click on the results
This gives rise to CTR, click-through-rate, which means
Online Metrics: CTR, CVR, CVS?
C T R = \frac { \text {Number of Clicks} } { \text {Number of Expressions} }
There are many other ways to also engage
... For example if we present items to buy and the user buys them, we can the conversion rate
C V R = \frac { \text {Number of Conversions} } { \text {Number of Expressions} }
Online Metrics: CTR, CVR, CVS?
But impression does not mean we viewed the item ... We also have

C V S = \frac { \ } { \ } \frac { \ } { \ } \left ( \begin{matrix} \text {Number of Views} \\ \text {or} \end{matrix} \right ) \\ C V S = \frac { \ } { \ } \frac { \ } { \ } \left ( \begin{matrix} \text {Number of Conversions} \\ \text {Number of Clicks} \end{matrix} \right ) \\ \intertext { \ } \text {For the above, we can use cookies to track if a user view}
C V S = \frac { \text {Number of Conversions} } { \text {Number of Views} }
For the above, we can use cookies to track if a user viewed the page, clicked, etc
For videos, we might also have average watch time or average completion rate
If we present k items per query, all of these metrics might be computed at different k so we can see the effect of results at the top and in the middle of the presented list
The idea is that these metrics are correlated to offline metrics, but the correlation is not always anywhere close to 1.0
Moreover, enough though these are easy to measure, we might be interested in more complicated metrics, such as average watch time not just click though rate

Design Youtube Video Search
Problem
So back to the concrete problem: Given a user and a textual search, we want to present the best videos to this user
Some assumptions
There are 1 M videos
There are 1 M users
We have lots of information about interactions
I.e. When a video is watched we know its name, if it was clicked, finished or what percentage was watched, we know the age of the video, what channel it is displayed in, etc
Problem: Nail down what is going on
Usually, in situations like these you want to ask some questions to clarify ... For example
Question: Is the input text-only, or can users search with an image or video?
Answer: Text queries only
Question: Does the platform serve more than videos?
Answer: Just videos
Question: Do I have information like video title and description?
Answer: Yes; you have this and also the the video content
Question: Do we have training data?
Answer: Yes, let's assume we have about 10 M positive pairs (video id, text query)
given a textual search
Problem: Nail down what is going on
Usually, in situations like this you want to ask some questions to clarify ... For example Video ID 1234
Question: Do we need to personalize results for different users given the same query?
Answer: No, but do the best you can
Summary goal: Design a search system for videos ... The input is a text query, the output is a list of videos that are relevant to the text query
What is our objective?
Our objective is to get the most relevant videos for a user given a textual search
Strategy and speed considerations
We have a problem ...
For a given search and video (+ features), we need to say which videos would be the top choices (top 10, ranked) and we need to do this fast
'Fast' means we need at a high level a quick way to map a query and some features to a top 10 list of videos
Finally, notice that this is not personalized - we have no user information to worry about
Figuring out how to represent the query and the videos
So given a query, we want to match videos which are similar to this query
How can we represent a query
How can we represent a video?
Representing the query
The query is text, and we know several ways to represent it
We can use Word2Vec - We take the embeddings for each token / word in the query and average over them
We can use SentenceBERT - We take the CLS token and use it to represent the sentence or we average over all the other tokens in the sentence
Which one is better?
Word2Vec - Very quick, but no semantic meaning ... Words like 'bank' will always have the same embeddings and if a sentence is 'a b c d' or 'd c a b' we get the same representation via averaging
BERT - Better, contextual ... But slower ... However, we can use variants of DistilBERT which are small and still give strong representations
Representing the videos
Videos have a text part and a video part
For the text part, we can use any of the methods we used for the query
For the video part, there are a few options, but we want to summarize videos via an embedding
One idea: consume the entire video, and create an embedding to summarize it
This idea uses 3D convolutions and Transformer models
This is expensive, as we need to consume the entire video
Representing the videos

At a high level, this grabs frames, processes each image with a CNN or some other Transformer, and then averages the embeddings of the frames to get the overall embedding for the video

If the video and the query are different, the inner product is low

Re-Ranking? What else?
In the last picture you see a ranking step
The idea of this type of design is that we have a huge number of videos to select, and we first want to focus on retrieval and high Recall so that we get a set of relevant videos for our query
Now, once we have a manageable set of size 100 (say), we can use a heavier model to refine our results with ranking ...
At the ranking stage, we focus on Precision as the key metric
Anything before we rank? What else?
The architecture we used was nice for a few reasons
Essentially, we used a BiEncoder for relevance - this is a Two-Tower architecture which produces embeddings such that a search embedding is close to the video embedding
This type of architecture is good because we can cache / save embedding and at runtime we just need the query embedding
A set of videos might be huge and a standard search O ( N ) to find the 10 best, but there are methods to do this much quicker in O (log N ) time
Candidate Generator and kNN
At this step, we need to find, for a search, the closest videos
Here, closest means the videos with the highest inner product to the user's embedding
Thus, we score videos via a score like q ⊺ v
Effectively, we want to find the k nearest neighboring videos given a user in terms of their dot product

The problem is that finding the nearest neighbors this way, referred to as maximum inner product search (MIPS) across one million videos can take prohibitively long when serving candidates in real time to user
Specifically, if there are | V | videos and each vector has dimensions d , finding the top k will be O ( | V | log kd ) (use a max heap)
So, we'll need a way to trade off the accuracy of the nearest neighbors search in favor of a reduced latency ... We need to get rid of the | V |
which will contain similar embeddings / vI
LSH kNN idea
This type of issue falls under the umbrella of approximate nearest neighbor search
The main idea of most approximate kNN algorithms revolves around breaking up the embedding space into regions, for example, locality sensitive hashing (LSH)
The main idea of LSH is to partition the embeddings by their similarities and then when searching for nearest neighbors, look only at the partition that the input example falls into
At runtime, this prevents the need to search through all of the video embeddings and instead isolates the search to a partition which will contain similar embeddings / videos
How does kNN work?
We have | V | videos and we can't find the top videos for a query embedding blindly because this will be O ( | V | ) which is too expensive
Approximate kNN algorithms try to reduce the above complexity to O (log | V | ) thereby allowing much faster retrieval
High level ideas?
Locality sensitive hashing would map each video to a hash ... At runtime, the query is projected to a bucket and we only look for videos inside of that bucket
Tree based methods also work, and they partition the space of videos into regions ... At runtime, the query is projected to a region and similarly we just consider videos in that region
Finally, we can also group videos by similarity via clustering ... At runtime, we look at the cluster the query belongs to and then just search that cluster
For all the ideas above, the methods are tested and available to you, so you can use things like FAISS or SCANN quite easily ... The error rates on these algorithms is quite low
Re-Ranking? What else?
Some ideas on the final ranker

At this step, we can also use a CrossEncoder so that for each candidate video we get a score when we feed in the pair ( q , v ) where q is the query v is the video ... We do this for all videos we need to consider

For example, if we have 100 videos we might break up the 100 into batches of size 10 feed these batches to servers which host the CrossEncoders and then get 100 scores and rank the videos based on these scores
Here, we batch since we want to distribute the load but this might not be necessary with the right model + GPU
I.e. if the model large, we might need this ... If it is small, we might not

Re-Ranking? What else?
Some ideas on the final ranker
For each video, we also know features such as: historical CTR EMA for 3, 5, 7 days, EMA of likes for 3, 5, 7 days, differences between EMAs, age, topic, etc
We could also have a MLP which takes in the query embedding, the video embedding, a set of normalized video features, and finally produces a score (maybe a probability of click)?
This is another idea but notice that we have a lot of features here but many fewer videos

There are other cases: we can have multiple rakers where the rankers get more and more 'heavy' (i.e. first we have a Logistic Regression, then an MLP / CrossEncoder hybrid but this last ranker runs on very few videos)
After ranking, we can even have another phase that boosts new videos up, ensures diversity, etc - these could be heuristic rules
This last step is crucial also!
What if we wanted personalization?
In this case, for two users who search the same query, we expect that the answers might not be the same ... I.e. We want to return answers that depend on the user ID
In this case, then, we need to build for each user a feature vector so that we can incorporate information into the model's ranking
One idea: We have a system such that for a search query we return a set of relevant videos ...
We could ...
Use our previous system to retrieve a set of relevant videos regardless of the user
At the ranking step, we can impute user features such as age, location, sex, historical CTR, historical logins etc and use these features to rank videos
But, ideally, we don't just do this at ranking time ... Ideally we have a set of user embeddings per user and we use this at retrieval time
What if we wanted personalization? More topics for next time ...
We'll need a way to figure out user embeddings
What about new users? This is the cold-start problem
For our previous analysis, we could have new videos but we just need to push these through the CNN and save the embeddings
Moreover, if we are not sure, we can use some sort of heuristic .. For example:
Find the topic of the video
Use the average video embedding across videos in this topic
What about bias?
What if the model is large?
What about click-bait videos?
What is Sparse search? I.e. How else can we search beyond just using embeddings and why should we even bother?

Getting a Personal Assistant
Is SFT enough?
Using SFT there are a few key limitations:
Data reflects human outputs, not preferences
You're training the model to mimic what a human might say, but not to choose among many plausible options based on what humans prefer
What about ambiguity and multiple good answers
Often, there's more than one good answer to a prompt
SFT only shows one per example, which may bias the model away from equally good or better answers
SFT doesn't capture what people like vs what's merely correct
Just because something is factually right doesn't mean it's helpful, kind, clear, or engaging
SFT doesn't optimize for human satisfaction or alignment with human values
Getting a Personal Assistant
For example, suppose we have an LLM and we've fine tuned it to generally add two numbers
... Suppose we want to build an assistant to help us do calculations and understand how to do them ... We have a question: 'What is 2 + 2*3?'
We might get two generations like below:
Generation 1: '8'
Generation 2: 'First, I want to use the laws of operations to calculate 2*3 and get 6. Then, I add 2 and get 8.'
Neither of the above is wrong, but we want to entice the model to generate like the second answer ... How might we do that?
So, to review, to get a personal assistant we need a few things
Step 1: Build a general LLM
For this part, we will just train a Transformer for next token prediction
To get this working, you process data so that generally ( y 0 , . . . , y t -1 ) is used as the input and ( y 1 , . . . , y t ) is used as the output
The above is generally now a Transformer decoder, but it can be any model; Transformers are used because you can scale and they do very well
For the above you still need to determine the right data to use
You need quality data ...
You still need to know the number of parameters vs your compute budget
You need to carefully do the optimization ...
Etc.
The idea of this step is we need a general model that understand language well
Step 2: Fine tune to your use case
Here, the idea is we want a model that understands that given a format of examples it should turn around and answer specifically the questions we address it

Recall the KL Divergence KL [ p || q ] = ∑ y p ( y ) log p ( y ) q ( y )
KL Divergence for any two distributions p and q
Recall the KL Divergence
K L [ p | | q ] = \sum _ { y } p ( y ) \log \frac { p ( y ) } { q ( y ) } = E _ { p } [ \log \frac { p ( y ) } { q ( y ) } ]
We have log is concave so generally by Jensen's nequality
E [ \log X ] \leq E [ \log X ]
for any random variable X
The above is just log[( a + b ) / 2] ≥ log a +log b 2 but more generally
We have
K L [ p | | q ] = \sum _ { y } p ( y ) \log \frac { p ( y ) } { q ( y ) } = E _ { p } [ \log \frac { p ( y ) } { q ( y ) } ] = - E _ { p } [ \log \frac { q ( y ) } { p ( y ) } ]
KL Divergence for any two distributions p and q
We have, by Jensen on f ( x ) = -log x :
K L = - E _ { p } [ \log \frac { q ( y ) } { p ( y ) } ] \geq - \log E _ { p } [ \frac { q ( y ) } { p ( y ) } ] = - \log \sum _ { y } q ( y ) = - \log 1 = 0
So, KL Divergence is a positive metric
It can be shown that KL is zero if an only if p and q are the same
Generally, we can use the KL Divergence as a type of 'regularizer' to make sure one distribution is 'close' to some base distribution

The above model is called Direct Preference Optimization
At a high level, this is a Reinforcement Learning alternative which has no modeled reward function
This encourages the model to assign higher probability to preferred outputs - no explicit reward function is needed, but the loss function implicitly reflects preference
DPO uses human preference data directly to optimize a contrastive loss between a preferred output and a less preferred one
... You could say the log-probabilities of preferred vs dispreferred outputs act as a proxy reward
DPO is effectively matching the behavior of an implicit reward function that ranks outputs based on preference

DPO Issues?
Issues with DPO?

Assumption Mismatch .. DPO is derived from a probabilistic model assuming humans make decisions based on a softmax over reward values. But real human preferences are often noisy and inconsistent. If the assumptions don't hold, the model can misinterpret the preference data

Implicit Reward Model ... DPO skips the explicit reward model used in RLHF and optimizes policy directly. This is great for simplicity but means you can't inspect, analyze, or reuse a separate reward model, which can be valuable for understanding model behavior or for multi-stage training setups ... I can't really see the reward even thought I know it is connected to r ∗

Sensitivity to Preference Quality ... DPO uses pairwise comparisons (e.g., A > B) to guide training. If those preferences are sparse, inconsistent, or biased, the optimization can go astray. There's no reinforcement loop to 'pull back' the model like in PPO (used in RLHF)

Less Control Over Output Distribution ... Because DPO skips reinforcement-style feedback loops, it may offer less control over certain safety and behavioral constraints that can be more finely tuned with RLHF (e.g., toxicity reduction via explicit reward shaping)

Lack of Exploration ... In RLHF (especially with PPO), the agent is encouraged to explore and learn from feedback. DPO, being a supervised-style method, does not explicitly explore alternative outputs-it just learns from the comparisons you give it
Why use RLHF?
Benefits of RLHF?
Explicit Reward Modeling ... RLHF gives you a separate reward model, which can be valuable for:
Auditing what the model 'likes'
Reusing it across models
Improving interpretability

Fine-Grained Control via Reward Tuning ... Want your model to be funny and safe and informative? RLHF lets you weight these reward components explicitly and dynamically adjust the mix ... There is no 'optimal' implicit reward

Better Performance in Complex or High-Stakes Domains ... In cases where getting nuanced behavior is critical (like legal or medical advice), RLHF might better capture and enforce complex preference structures (+ rewards)

Proven Track Record ... Many high-profile models (like ChatGPT and Claude) use RLHF because it's a mature and battle-tested method. If you're aiming for production-level robustness, RLHF is still the gold standard
Is there anything other than DPO?
Alternatives to DPO
There are a few DPO alternatives ... Ask Chat GPT for some papers!
Big idea is to change some of the final optimization but sometimes there is no nice analytic derivation ...

Some ideas to consider
What do you embed? Documents? Chunks?
Do you use metadata?
How do you chunk?
To retrieve chunks, you can use a BiEncoder
Offline, documents are chunked and the chunks are embedded by the BiEncoder and stored in a database
At runtime the query is encoded and the chunks with the highest inner product against the query are retrieved
For a query, you get the documents which have the highest cosine similarity in the embedding space
Using brute force, this is O ( N )
However, we can do this fast in O (log N )
Ranking can be tuned ... For example, to get chunks that are temporally fresh at the top, you can rank newer chunks before older chunks by boosting using a factor
RAG - Chunking Analysis / Strategy is its own niche field
What to do?
Tune the chunk length, but remember each chunk gets an embedding and only finitely any tokens can go into an LLM to get an embedding
Pick a specific piece of text in the document and then use that as the query
Q - Do you get a subset of this text as the closest chunk C ? Can also have metrics using Levenshtein distance:
Recall = | Q |-LD ( C , D ) | Q | ▶ Precision = | C |-LD ( C , Q ) | C |
Furthermore, we can also do Semantic chunking, whereby we (for example) split on sentences and then merge greedily sentences that are close to each other
We can also use NSP from BERT to say C 1 and C 2 should be merged?
Can we have a custom BiEncoder model? How do we determine the cutoff score to merge two chunks?
Either of the above is possible and it matters ...

Scaling Laws
After 2020, Transformer style Language Models kept getting bigger and bigger Training Tokens
GPT1, GPT2 and GPT3 were each essentially the same architecture but they were different in the total parameter count and the amount of data they were trained on
Other models by other teams beyond Open AI were introduced ... For example, Alphabet released Gopher
Question: Given a compute budget, how many parameters should the model have and how much data should it be trained on
Optimal here means lowest Training ( ∼ Test) perplexity or loss L
Scaling Laws are everywhere
Roughly speaking, a scaling law connects the Error / Loss with the size of the model of the size of the data we have
Let's do an example
Suppose we have a distribution with constant variance σ 2 and are given data { x 1 , . . . , x N } and want to find out an estimator for the mean µ
Typically, we can use ˆ µ = x 1+ ... + xN N to estimate µ
Scaling Laws are everywhere
The Mean Squared Error (MSE) between the estimator and the true parameter is
M S E = E ( ( \hat { \mu } - \mu ) ^ { 2 } )
We have E (ˆ µ ) = µ so that Bias = 0 and because this is unbiased we have
E ( ( \hat { \mu } - \mu ) ^ { 2 } ) = B i a s ^ { 2 } + V a r ( \hat { \mu } ) = V a r ( \hat { \mu } )
Simplifying, we get
M S E = E ( ( \hat { \mu } - \mu ) ^ { 2 } ) = \frac { \sigma ^ { 2 } } { N }
Form here, we get
\log ( M S E ) = 2 \log \sigma - \log N
This is a Scaling Law: We are connecting the Error with the size of the data set we have, N
Scaling Laws are everywhere
The above tells us a lot
Specifically, given we know that the log expected Error is for size 1000, we can use the above to figure out what the error would be for size N = 1 M
In this case, it is easy to figure this out directly, but this is not always the case so figuring out such a 'best fit line' is crucial

At a high level, the authors establish empirical rules which connect the Error or Loss with the model parameter count and the training dataset size Jurassic (Lieber et al., 2021) Chinchilla 178 Billion 300 Billion

Specifically, having established the true line, Chinchilla is trained using the same approximate budget ( = parameters × tokens) as Gopher and GPT-3 but notice that it has much fewer parameters and does much better on downstream tasks
Why does this matter?
Smaller models have less inference latency
Smaller models are easier to load and use
Smaller models are 'easier to train'; we just need lots of data but less GPUs
Also, it is nice to understand when a model is 'baked' ... When should we stop training a model?
Scaling Laws
Key question: What is compute cost?
Compute Cost = FLOPs
FLOPs: Floating Point Operations per second
Sort of like big O notation, used to measure work
Adding two floats costs 1
Multiplying two floats costs 1
What else?
FLOPs Calculations for Matrices
Suppose we have two vectors a and b of dimension d
How many FLOPs do we need to compute a ⊺ b ?
a ⊺ b = ∑ d i =1 a i b i
We need d multiplications
We need d -1 additions
So, we need about 2 d -1 FLOPs to compute an inner product
What if we have two matrices A and B of dimensions m × d and d × n
What is the compute cost of AB
Each row of A has dimension d and each column of B has d elements
So, for the first column of B with all rows of A we need m (2 d -1) FLOPs since there are m rows
But B has n columns
So, we need approximately mn (2 d -1) FLOPs
How else can we use this?
Scaling Laws - Forward Pass and Backward Pass FLOPs
Suppose we have a Transformer
Suppose we train it on D tokens and suppose it has N total parameters
A Neural Network is a bunch of matrices ...
Computations like the previous ones can give us several results
For derivations, see the References to this lecture and the Chinchilla Appendix
Generally, 2 * Forward FLOPs ∼ Backward FLOPs
Total FLOPs for Forward Pass in the Transformer: ∼ 2 ND
Total FLOPs for Backward Pass in the Transformer: ∼ 4 ND
Total FLOPs ∼ 6 ND

Optimizing N and D over FLOPs
So, in terms of FLOPS work, the Backward Pass has approximately twice the number of FLOPs as the Forward Pass
Given a Transformer Language Model model with N parameters and trained on D tokens, we can estimate the number of FLOPs needed as 6 ND
A goal, therefore, might be to find
N ^ { * } ( C ) , D ^ { * } ( C ) = \arg \min _ { \{ F L O P s ( N , D ) = C \} } \mathcal { L } ( N , D )
In the above, L is the (Test) loss from the model and C is the compute budget, which is the total number of FLOPs
Optimization Problem and Implications
We want to fix C and solve
N ^ { * } ( C ) , D ^ { * } ( C ) = \arg \min _ { \{ F L O P s ( N , D ) = C \} } \mathcal { L } ( N , D )
This is a very hard non analytical problem. At a high level, the authors do this purely empirically by varying N and D and looking at the loss when FLOPs ( N , D ) = C
We have optimality in this setting for ( N ∗ D ∗ ) if F ( N ∗ , D ∗ ) = C and
\mathcal { L } ( N _ { * } , D _ { * } ) < \mathcal { L } ( N , D ) \ \forall N , D , F L O P s ( N , D ) = C

Softmax
Whenever we have a vector of values, we might be interested in the maximum one: Which index attains the maximum?
For example if z = ( z 1 , . . . , z K ) then perhaps z 3 is largest
The softmax is a 'soft' approximation of the max, where we turn each component of z into a probability
Specifically, this is done by saying
Softmax
The softmax function turns each value into a probability
The maximum component of the vector a is the same as the maximum component of a = softmax ( z )
We have
\arg \max _ { j } z _ { j } = \arg \max _ { j } a _ { j }
But, unlike the max which is nondifferentiable, the softmax is differentiable
We have

\frac { d s o f t \max } { d z _ { k } } ( z ) = s o f t \max ( z ) _ { k } ( 1 - s o f t \max ( z ) _ { k } ) = \frac { e ^ { z _ { k } } ( \sum _ { j = 1 , j \neq k } ^ { d } e ^ { z _ { j } } ) } { ( \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { K } e ^ { z _ { j } } ) ^ { 2 } }
Softmax is a generalization of σ ( z ) = 1 1+ e -z = e z 1+ e z

Cross Entropy and Softmax in General
When we have a model that takes x and outputs a label y , we might have that y is in a class with more than 2 values
If for a fixed x ( i ) the model outputs a set of probabilities per class given by a ( i ) = ( a ( i ) 1 , . . . , a ( i ) K ) when a typical loss to consider is
\ell ( \theta ) = - \frac { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { K } o n e H o t ( y ^ { ( i ) } ) _ { k } \log a _ { k } ^ { ( i ) } } { N }
Here, the oneHot ( y ( i ) ) ∈ R K is the one-hot representation of the i th response value: a vector of 1/0 values where only one value is 1 and all others are zero; oneHot ( y ( i ) ) y ( i ) = 1
Here, a ( i ) is a vector of probabilities and ∑ K k =1 a ( i ) k = 1 and a ( i ) k ≥ 0 ∀ k
A good model is such that if the true label is at index k so oneHot ( y ) k = 1 then a k ∼ 1 and all others probabilities are small
But how do we get a model taking in x ( i ) to output a vector a ( i ) which is a probability?
Cross Entropy - Review of how this works in practice
Typically, a model outputs a set of logits z ∈ R K which are real values
After, we apply a softmax to get probabilities a = softmax ( z )
Then, these values define a loss ℓ which we minimize
Note that for a training point with oneHot ( y ( i ) ) k = 1 the above reduces to -log a ( i ) k
Since 0 ≤ a ( i ) k ≤ 1 a good model will be such that it's parameters are adjusted so that a ( i ) k ∼ 1
When we optimize ℓ , the model parameters are adjusted so that on average this behavior happens on the Training set { ( x ( i ) , y ( i ) ) } N i =1
If we do this too much on the training set, then we overfit and need to deal with this problem
Cross Entropy σ vs Softmax again
Final note: If we have 2 classes, we can use Binary or regular Cross Entropy
Model outputs a = softmax ( z ) ∈ R 2 then we use the one-hot of oneHot ( y ) ∈ R 2 and consider minimizing
\ell = - ( o n e H o t ( y ) _ { 1 } \log a _ { 1 } + o n e H o t ( y ) _ { 2 } \log a _ { 2 } )
Model outputs a = σ ( z ) ∈ R then we use y ∈ R = 1/0 and consider minimizing
\ell = - ( y \log \sigma ( z ) + ( 1 - y ) \log 1 - \sigma ( z ) )
These are the same thing!

XOR: Why we need Neural Networks
We can make Logistic Regression models where we take two features x 1 and x 2 and define a new feature x 3 = x 1 ∗ x 2 and try and estimate y ∼ θ 1 x 1 + θ 2 x 2 + θ 3 x 3
The above model is still a Logistic Regression and linear in its parameters; there are still things basic (Convex) models like Logistic Regression cannot do
When the loss is convex (i.e. ℓ ( θ ) ∼ θ 2 ) and we minimize ℓ ( θ ) we know that any local optima is global (Theorem), so these type of problems are 'easy'
There are many algorithms to optimize convex problems, and these algorithms have nice guarantees ...
However, we want to look here in detail at one example where Logistic Regression cannot do well
XOR: The Problem
Consider two variables x 1 , x 2 that take on values of 1 or 0
Set y = x 1 + x 2 -2 x 1 x 2
The y is known as XOR and the values are as follows
If x 1 = 1 and x 2 = 1 then y = 0
If x 1 = 1 and x 2 = 0 then y = 1
If x 1 = 0 and x 2 = 1 then y = 1
If x 1 = 0 and x 2 = 0 then y = 0
Problem: Given 4 data points { ( x ( i ) , y ( i ) ) } 4 i =1 that represent the 4 points above, can we find a classifier that can correctly classify these points?
At a high level, we need a model that has
For example, we want p ( y = 1 | x 1 = 1 , x 2 = 0) > 1 / 2

Let's try a linear classifier like Logistic Regression

We consider z = θ ⊺ x + β ∈ R
a = σ ( z ) ∈ R is the probability that the point should be classified as 1
What do we need? For example when x = (1 , 0) we know y = 1
In this case, we want σ ( z ) to be more than 1 2 since we want the probability of being 1 to be high
On the other hand, if x = (1 , 1) we know y = 0 so then we want σ ( z ) to less than 1 2 , since we want the probability of 0 (which is 1 -σ ( z )) to be high
XOR: Getting restrictions on the parameters
Consider the case x = (1 , 0) for which we know y = 1
We have θ ⊺ x + β = θ 1 + β and we want σ ( θ 1 + β ) > 1 2
This is the same as 1 1+ e -( θ 1 + β ) > 1 2

\theta _ { 1 } + \beta > 0

If x = (1 , 1) then y = 0 so θ 1 + θ 2 + β < 0
If x = (0 , 0) then y = 0 so β < 0
We get e ( θ 1+ β ) > 1

So we have
If x = (1 , 0), y = 1 we need σ ( θ 1 + β ) > 1 / 2 so
\theta _ { 1 } + \beta > 0
If x = (0 , 1), y = 1 we need σ ( θ 2 + β ) > 1 / 2 so
\theta _ { 2 } + \beta > 0
If x = (1 , 1), y = 0 we need 1 -σ ( θ 1 + θ 2 + β ) > 1 / 2 so

For example, d ℓ d β [2]

Derivatives of Nonlinearities
What if we used a [1] = ReLU ( z [1] ) = max { z [1] , 0 } or a [1] = tanh ( z [1] ) in the above?
For f ( z ) = ReLU ( z ) you can show that
The gradient is 1 if z > 0
0 otherwise
Succinctly this can be written as the indicator function dReLU dz = 1 (0 , ∞ ) ( z )
Here 1 A ( z ) is 1 if z ∈ A and zero otherwise
Technically, this is not a gradient, it is a sub-gradient, but this does not matter: pretty much everything 'just works'
For f ( z ) = tanh ( z ) you can show that
dtanh dz = 1 -tanh 2 ( z )
For f ( z ) = σ ( z ) we know that
d σ dz = σ ( z )(1 -σ ( z ))
Why ReLU is better than σ
What happens to the derivatives of σ ( z ) when z is large ( ∞ )? What if z is small (-∞ )?
We have σ ( z ) ∼ 1 for z very large and σ ( z ) ∼ 0 for z very small
But we also have d σ dz = σ ( z )(1 -σ ( z ))
What this means is that for these extreme values, d σ dz = 0 and this implies that if z [1] is extreme then
This is a problem: we will make no progress on this parameter when we do gradient ascent
The update rule is θ new = θ old + ρ d ℓ d θ = θ old in this case
This type of issue, where you get zero gradients and don't learn, is usually called the 'saturation' or 'vanishing gradient' problem for Deep Neural Networks
Why ReLU is better than tanh
What happens to the derivatives of tanh( z ) when z is large ( ∞ )? What if z is small (-∞ )?
We have tanh ( z ) ∼ 1 for z very large and also tanh ( z ) ∼ -1 for z very small
Now we have dtanh dz = 1 -tanh 2 ( z ) so for extreme values we get a 0 gradient and the same problem happens as for σ
We'll see some methods later which aim to fix this

But what is a Convolution?
In math, convolution is usually written as
( f * g ) ( n ) = \sum _ { m = - M } ^ { M } f [ n - m ] g [ m ]
The idea above is to run some filter g across a function f and get a new function 'mixing' f and g
At a high level, this is a type of moving inner product where we cross g with all the possible shifted f values
Result has size 3 × 3
Example of a Convolution for Images
Images are usually stored as (data) boxes of a certain width, height, and depth (channel)
Assume the depth is 1 (back-white); 'run a filter over the data over with a certain stride' 2
To note:
Data has size 5 × 5
Filter has size 3 × 3
We move the filter across each column 1 step at a time and when we come to the end columns we go 1 row down and repeat
Result has size 3 × 3
Effects of Convolution
Note that the original data was shrunk in dimensions
Note that we moved the filter across 1 column at a time
We could have moved this 2 (or more) columns at a time, but, then we'd have a smaller result
The filter is applied to a sub-image of the exact same dimension as the filter (3 × 3), but we could have added 'padding' with this padded image to get a result of the same size as before
Stride is 1 on the left, 2 on the right

More than 1 Channel? Bias?
If you have data of the form H × W × C then a general filter will be of dimensions f × f × C
For example, if you don't pad and have a stride of 1, each filter will give a result of size H -f +1 × W -f +1 × 1
If you have D filters, the result is H -f +1 × W -f +1 × D where each channel dimension came from a filter
Each filter has f × f × C +1 parameters: you inner product with the data and then you also add a bias to get the scalar final result
Upshot: A filter is a small box and also scalar bias

What about CNN models for NLP?
Given a sentence and a representation of each token in that text (one-hot, word vectors, etc.) how can we aggregate subsequences and get representations for each subsequence?
Example: 'I went to the Apple store to buy the cool new laptop.'
How can we summarize the above by using groups of length 3?
There are 10 such contiguous groups of words for the above sentence
They are: { I went to } , { went to the } , { to the Apple } . . . { cool new laptop }
Main Idea of CNN for NLP
The phrases we make, we do not care if they are grammatical
Or linguistically valid
Or cognitively plausible
What we care is trying to figure out if we can understand some sort of summary context from this information

features across { a , b , c } , { b , c , d } , { c , d , e } , { d , e , f } , { e , f , g } , . . .

Big Benefit of CNN: We can apply multiple filters in parallel!
One of the big benefits of CNN is we don't need to wait for one filter to process data through another filter
On the same layer level, we can apply all the filters in parallel and then put these together to get the result

Optimization
We usually have a problem like min θ ( L ( θ )) where L is some loss function
L usually depends on your problem and on the training data
L ( θ ) = | y -θ |
L ( θ ) = -∑ N i =1 ∑ K k =1 y ( i ) k log a ( i ) k N
More complicated examples: Skip-Gram, CBOW, CNN
There are recipes we use to optimize these problems

An example of gradient descent
to the left than down (note:
Issue 1: We want gradient updates to be smooth, so we make progress to the minimum).
Issue 2: We want to balance the way we move, even if a gradient is larger with respect to one dimension vs another
Stochastic Gradient Descent is the most popular method to optimize min θ ( L ( θ ))
Divide your training set into batches B 1 , . . . , B T which cover your entire data
Compute the gradient of L on these batches (i.e. assume the full data is just batch B t and consider L Bt and optimize over this set)
Update the parameters: θ t = θ t -1 -ν ∂ L Bt ∂θ ( θ t -1 )
∂ L B ∂θ acts like a proxy for the (expensive, full) ∂ L ∂θ
