SGD: Randomization Helps
SGD vs Gradient Descent
Figure (a): Batch Gradient Descent ( B = N
Figure (b): SGD ( B < N
If we initialize wrong, as in case 2-a below, standard Gradient Descent goes to a bad place
Idea: Randomization helps and can allow the optimizer get to the right place even if we initialize wrong (i.e. 2-b)
SGD: Batch size and noisy estimates
Batches can be of size B = 1, 256, 128, etc.
The smaller the batch, the noisier the estimate
But, the smaller the batch, the more gradient updates (learning) you can do
Since we compute ∂ L B ∂θ over batches, this is a noisy estimate for the gradient of ∂ L ∂θ over the entire training set
What are some methods that deal with this noise?
Momentum: Faster convergence with less variance in gradient updates
We keep track of a momentum term
m _ { t } = \beta m _ { t - 1 } + ( 1 - \beta ) \frac { \partial \mathcal { L } _ { B _ { t } } } { \partial \theta } ( \theta _ { t - 1 } )
The idea is ∂ L Bt ∂θ is choppy, but m t is smoother
The update would now be, for batch t , update via
\theta _ { t } = \theta _ { t - 1 } - \nu m _ { t }
Note m t is a function of the past parameters θ t -1 , θ t -2 , . . .
As we progress through more and more iterations, we put less and less weight on older gradient estimates, but we never forget old gradients and updates are 'slower and smoother'
Momentum
b)
Loss, L|ф]
Momentum adds smoothness to the loss trajectory
With momentum, the gradient estimate has less variance, we don't trash around in different directions
Figure (a): SGD (batch size B =1)
Figure (b): Momentum
The effective learning rate increases if all these gradients are aligned over multiple iterations but decreases if the gradient direction repeatedly changes as the terms in the sum cancel out
Nesterov Momentum: updating quicker
The momentum term can be considered a coarse prediction of where the SGD algorithm will move next
Nesterov accelerated momentum computes the gradient at this point rather than at the current point

Issues with SGD and normalization
How do we make progress in both directions?

Gradient descent with a fixed step size has the following undesirable property: it makes large adjustments to parameters associated with large gradients (where perhaps we should be more cautious) and small adjustments to parameters associated with small gradients (where perhaps we should explore further)

When the gradient of the loss surface is much steeper in one direction than another, it is difficult to choose a learning rate that (i) makes good progress in both directions and (ii) is stable (figures 6.9a-b)

Towards Adam: Normalized Gradients
Straightforward approach: is to normalize the gradients so that we move a fixed distance (governed by the learning rate) in each direction
m t = ∂ L Bt ∂θ ( θ t -1 )
v t = ( ∂ L Bt ∂θ ( θ t -1 )) 2
The term v t is the point wise squared gradient, and the positive root of this is used to normalize the gradient itself, so all that remains is the sign in each coordinate direction
θ t = θ t -1 -ν mt √ vt + ϵ
The result is that the algorithm moves a fixed distance ν along each coordinate, where the direction is determined by whichever way is downhill (see Figure (c) in the previous slide)
This simple algorithm makes good progress in both directions
But, it will not converge unless it happens to land exactly at the minimum
Instead, it will bounce back and forth around the minimum

So just always use Adam?
When it came out, people got excited with Adam and its performance
After some time, people discovered that for some datasets Adam does not get to the optimal place, SGD is necessary
The initial Adam paper had a mistake in its convergence proof
How do you fix it? One idea was to run Adam and then switch to SGD after ... See the references
Generally, Adam is a good optimizer to try, but you might need to do other things like the above
4 More Tricks
Schedulers: Adjust the learning rate in a smart way
Gradient Accumulation: How to use 1 GPU like you have 4
Distributed Data Parallelism: One way to use 4 GPUs
Warm Up: Don't just go from random parameters to better all at once, do it slowly so that the model is not 'shocked'
Schedulers
At a high level, batch gradient updates are of the sort
\theta _ { n e w } = \theta _ { o l d } - \nu \frac { \partial \mathcal { L } _ { B _ { t } } } { \partial \theta } ( \theta _ { o l d } )
This means that we can have

\frac { \theta _ { n e w } - \theta _ { o l d } } { \theta _ { o l d } } = \nu \frac { \frac { \partial \mathcal { L } _ { B _ { t } } } { \partial \theta } ( \theta _ { o l d } ) } { \theta _ { o l d } }
Assume that || θ new -θ old θ old || ∼ 1 1000 so this is fairly stable
This means that
- 3 \sim \log _ { 1 0 } \nu + \log _ { 1 0 } \frac { \ s t d ( \frac { \partial \mathcal { L } _ { B _ { t } } } { \partial \theta } ( \theta _ { o l d } ) ) } { \ s t d ( \theta _ { o l d } ) }
So this means
If log 10 ν +log 10 std ( ∂ L Bt ∂θ ( θ old )) std ( θ old ) < -3, increase the learning rate
If log 10 ν +log 10 std ( ∂ L Bt ∂θ ( θ old )) std ( θ old ) > -3, decrease the learning rate
Schedulers
Ideas like the above are part of a larger theory of learning rate schedulers; the goal is to help learning when it stalls
Popular schedulers in PyTorch such as ExponentialLR or ReduceLROnPlateau help change the learning rate as needed if some criterion is met
ExponentialLR: Every epoch, lower the learning rate by γ
ReduceLROnPlateau: If Val Accuracy has not gone lower for 3 iterations, lower the learning rate by γ
You can even make your own (i.e. the previous slide) ...
Gradient Accumulation
Problem: you want to use a large batch size for optimization, but you have 1 GPU
You find that for your model, you can fit just 2 training samples per GPU
What can you do?
You can, for example just use batch size 2 ... But each of these estimates are noisy
Large Batch Sizes
Key Benefits of Large Batch Sizes
Computational Efficiency:
Parallelism: Larger batches allow for better utilization of hardware resources, especially GPUs which excel at parallel matrix multiplication and other operations

Reduced Communication Overhead: Your entire data is on the CPU and as you optimize you move it to the GPU (see above). The overhead of transferring data between the CPU and GPU is relatively fixed. With larger batches, the data transfer cost per example becomes lower
Enhanced Convergence: Large batch sizes allow for better utilization of parallel processing hardware (GPUs/TPUs), reducing the time taken per epoch
Large Batch Sizes
Key Benefits of Large Batch Sizes
Improved Generalization?

Noisier Gradient Estimates: Smaller batches can introduce more noise into the gradient estimates and generalize better (See later) ; Batch Norm and learning rate warming can help mimic this even with large batch sizes
Reduced Variance: Larger batches can reduce the variance of the gradient estimates; this can lead to smoother and more consistent updates to the model parameters
Larger Steps: Larger batches allow for taking larger steps in the optimization process. This can accelerate convergence, especially in the early stages of training
One idea: start with a large batch size and then decrease it (LLAMA 3.1 used this)
Gradient Accumulation
OK, so I want to use a large batch but I'm on Colab and I have 1 GPU
One idea: Gradient Accumulation ...
Process K batches each of size B sequentially
For each batch, compute the loss gradient but do not do an update step
Instead, accumulate the K gradients by computing the gradient for a batch and sum with the previously computed gradient
Once you've processed K batches, then do a gradient step
Idea is now the gradient step is as if we've used a batch of size B ∗ K
Gradient Accumulation
Example: I want to use a batch size of 128 but I can only fit a batch size of 32 on my GPU (data + model)
Take 4 batches, each of size 32 and feed each one sequentially through the model and then get a gradient on each batch
Each loss gradient is a sum of error terms divided by 32, this is since a loss is the penalty summed across the 32 samples divided by L = S 32
To get what we want, multiply what we have by 32 128
We want the gradient of S 1+ S 2+ S 3+ S 4 128 but we have the gradients Si 32
This is since
What if you have multiple GPUs?
What would I do if I had 4 GPUs and each could fit the model and a batch of maximum size 32?
The idea now is something called Distributed Data Parallelism
Idea: make a copy of each model, one on each GPU
For a batch of size 128, split it into 4 chunks of size 32, and feed a batch of size 32 to each GPU
Compute the gradient of the model on each GPU - note the model is assumed to be the same on each GPU
Once we have the 4 computed gradients, accumulate them as before to get a gradient which effectively is of size 128
Put this gradient onto each GPU
Apply the same gradient step to each model on each GPU
Now, as before, each GPU has the same model but we've done a gradient step of size 128 effectively and not 32 ....
Distributed Data Parallelism is a library in PyTorch you can leveredge
So do you always want to use a large batch?
Rule of thumb: smaller batch sizes generalize better
Large Batch sizes converge to sharp minima where while small batches converge to flat minima

If small perturbations the optima vector cause a lot of data misclassification, the minimizer is sharp; a small movement away from the optimal parameters causes a large increase in the loss function. In contrast, flat minima have accuracy that remains nearly constant under small parameter perturbations
So what do I do? Use small batch size?
What to do?
batch_64 Ir_0.02
batch_32_Ir_0.01
batch_ 128_Ir_0.04
Generally speaking, you need to try out experiments with the batch size and see what happens
There is no clean recipe always
For example, people use learning rate warm up to get large batch training to still generalize well
Notice both Batch Size and Ir are increasing by 2 every time
Validation
Warm up?
Idea is, we want to use a learning rate of 0 . 1
However, we don't want this all at once
Instead, increase the learning rate gradually from 0 to 0.1 in a few optimization epochs
Once we get to 0.1, then dampen it
Analytic Example: lr = α min { 1 √ t , t √ 10 3 }
For t ≤ 10 we use α t √ 10 3 (increase to α )
For t > 10 we use α 1 √ t (decrease once at α )

Anything else about small batch size?
▶ From GoodFellow's Deep Learning (2016):
Small batches can offer a regularizing effect (Wilson and Martinez, 2003), perhaps due to the noise they add to the learning process
Generalization error is often best for a batch size of 1
Training with such a small batch size might require a small learning rate to maintain stability because of the high variance in the estimate of the gradient
The total runtime can be very high as a result of the need to make more steps, both because of the reduced learning rate and because it takes more steps to observe the entire training set

BatchNorm
We optimize a Neural Network over batches
At a high level, for each element in a batch we feed it into a nonlinearity, so we get y = σ ( x )
What if x is large or small?
As before, we have that if x is large or small, σ ( x ) will be 0 or 1, and this might affect the gradient of parameters in lower layers
This is since d σ ( x ) dx = σ ( x )(1 -σ ( x ))
BatchNorm
We optimize a Network over batches and batches can change
A batch might change distribution, so for example x feeding into σ might be all over the place
The loss will be very bouncy
Idea: Normalize the data before feeding into the nonlinearity!
BatchNorm
For each batch B , compute µ B and σ 2 B which are the mean and variance of the different components of x in the batch B (these are vectors, of the same dimension as x )

For example, if B = { x 1 , . . . , x m } and each x i ∈ R d , then so are µ B and σ 2 B but each component of these vectors is the mean / variance of the respective components of all batch vectors x b , i.e. ( µ B )( j ) = ∑ m i =1 xi ( j ) m
Before we feed into σ , normalize each vector x ∈ B so that we use ˆ x = x -µ B √ σ 2 B + ϵ
Now, feed this normalized ˆ x into σ : less chance we'll get extremes!
But, what if we want to allow the network to learn the scaling and off-centering?
Introduce two (vector-valued) parameters γ , β
Use γ ˆ x + β as the input to σ
Maybe γ = 1 and β = 0, but let the network learn that
BatchNorm
So the process goes as follows
Fix a batch B = { x 1 , . . . , x m } and compute µ B = mean ( x B ) and σ B = stdev ( x B )

\mu _ { B } & = \text {mean} ( x _ { \mathcal { B } } ) \\ \sigma _ { \mathcal { B } } & = \text {stdev} ( x _ { \mathcal { B } } ) \\ \hat { x } & = \frac { x - \mu _ { \mathcal { B } } } { \sqrt { \sigma _ { \mathcal { B } } ^ { 2 } + \epsilon } } \\ y & = \gamma \hat { x } + \beta
Feed y to a nonlinearity
Basically, before you run the data through the nonlinearity, apply a batch normalization!

BatchNorm: Training and Inference
At training time, we compute µ B and σ 2 B for each batch and use these statistics in gradient computations
At inference time, we can recompute these statistics over the entire training set and use these as normalizations
Or, we can use some sort of running estimate (momentum!)
For example, if we process mini-batches B 1 , B 2 , . . . , B T in sequence, we can define a mean estimate as µ B = µ B 1 , then µ B = αµ B 2 +(1 -α ) µ B etc
We can use µ B at inference time
Same for σ 2 B
Be careful : the behavior of this layer depends on what state your model is in - if it is in eval or train mode!
LayerNorm
For NLP applications, usually we have batches of sentences and each sentence has different tokens and different lengths
Each token has an embedding
We can't really take a mean across all embeddings in the batch, since different embeddings are in different sentences
We'd lose context if we did this (imagine we have to pad short sentences - those 0's would affect the means)
For NLP, a batch is of dimension B × T × d
Before, a batch was of dimension B × d or B × H × W × C and the statistics were of dimension d or H × W × C
For NLP, it does not make sense to get statistics across T × d since we go between sentences which can be very different
LayerNorm
A better way is to normalize each word's (or token's) embedding
Thus, µ d and σ 2 d are the mean and variance across all components of a particular token embedding (these are scalars and we use broadcasting to do the calculations below)
This method does not depend on the batch size
We normalize ˆ e = e -µ d √ σ 2 d + ϵ
Finally, we apply γ and β as before (again, these are vectors) to allow the model to learn the transformation
We use γ ˆ e + β as the final representation of LayerNorm

Drop Out
The aim of dropout is to make sure the model does not overtrain too much
At a high level, for each batch we zero out a random proportion (20 %, 30 %) of the neurons
This effectively zeros out the parameters for these neurons
For a certain weight W , we effectively use D ∗ W where D is a Bernoulli random variable that is 1/0 with probability 1 -p and p

Drop Out
You can also think dropout as making the model learn as much as it can in this weakened state: do the best with what you have, don't get too comfortable using one set of nodes
This makes the network less dependent on any given hidden unit
We also encourage the weights to have smaller magnitudes so that the change in the function due to the presence or absence of the hidden unit is reduced
Drop Out
Be careful : the behavior of this layer (like Batch Norm) depends on what state (train or eval) your model is in!
Specifically, at inference time for a weight W , we use
as the weight of the layer
At training time, we use D ∗ W

Introduction to Language Models
Language Modeling is one of the oldest problems in NLP
Nowadays, language models are usually termed 'Foundation' Models since people are starting to realize that solving Language Modeling leads to solving other things
At a high level, we'd like to be able to construct plausible sentences, sensible sequences ( y 1 , . . . , y T ) with p ( y 1 , y 2 , . . . , y T ) high
This can be done with n-gram count based models (we will see one soon), CNNs (touched on this before), and even a standard MLP (we will see this also)
Finally, we will see how this is done with RNNs and we will find out how to 'generate' from an Language Model a sentence
Language Models: Why?
Language models in some sense allow us to understand how words follow each other and have many applications
Given a question, tell me the answer (identify the answer in some context)
Given the start of a sentence, tell me the next few words (generate text)
From a language model, we might extract contextual word representations (vectors) and apply them to other things - we'll see this later
Etc.
The vocabulary set V
First, we will define V to be the set of all words in the language
For example, this can be V = { the, dog, laughs, saw, barks, cat, . . . }
Assume V can be quite large
Assume V is finite
Given this corpus, we'd like to estimate the parameters of a Language Model
A sentence is a sequence of words
where T ≥ 1 and x t ∈ V for t ∈ { 1 , . . . , T -1 }
Language Models
We assume that x T is a special symbol STOP which is not in V
Depending on the model, we also have a symbol START which we will use; this is also not in V
This will typically be for x 0 or x -1 as we will see
So: all sentences begin with START and end in STOP
A sentence ( x 1 x 2 . . . x T -1 ) can then have the padded form ( x 0 , x 1 , . . . , x T ) or ( x -2 , x -1 , x 0 , x 1 , . . . , x T ) Etc.
Some example sentences are as follows
START the dog barks STOP
START the STOP START STOP
START START cat cat cat STOP
START the cat saw the dog STOP
Notice: Some sentences make more sense than others! Good sentences should have a higher 'score'
Language Models: The probability is the score
Given a vocabulary V there is an infinite set of sentences S of the form x 0 , x 1 . . . x T
Each sentence has a probability p ( x 0 . . . x T ) and a assume that ∑ x ∈S p ( x ) = 1
The above defines a Language Model
For example
Assume we have a bunch of sentences in a training corpus
For each sentence, define p ( x ) = count ( x ) |S| , where count ( x ) is the number of times we saw the sentence x in the corpus
Obviously, many sentences will have a probability 0, and this is a bad model
How can we do better? How can we say one model is better than another?
Markov Language Models
Markov Language Models are some of the oldest models whose goal is to do better than the above
To start, let is look at p ( x 0 , . . . , x T ) again and apply Bayes' rule in its 'chain rule' way
Recall the chain rule:
p ( x , y ) = p ( y | x ) p ( x )
p ( x , y , z ) = p ( z | y , x ) p ( x , y ) = p ( z | y , x ) p ( y | x ) p ( x )
p ( x , y , z , w ) = p ( w | z , y , x ) p ( z | y , x ) p ( y | x ) p ( x )
Etc.
If we do this over and over, we get
Markov Language Models
We generally have
p ( x _ { 0 } , \dots , x _ { T } ) \sim \prod _ { t = 1 } ^ { T } p ( x _ { t } | x _ { t - 1 } , \dots , x _ { 0 } )
In Markov Models, we assume that p ( x t | x t -1 , . . . , x 0 ) depends on the past in a finite way
This is the Markov Assumption: the future depends on the the immediate past, where 'immediate' can mean 1/2/3 Etc time steps
Bigram Model: p ( x t | x t - 1 , . . . , x 1 ) = p ( x t | x t - 1 )
Trigram Model: p ( x t | x t - 1 , . . . , x 1 ) = p ( x t | x t - 1 , x t - 2 )
Fourgram Model: p ( x t | x t - 1 , . . . , x 1 ) = p ( x t | x t - 1 , x t - 2 , x t - 3 )
Etc
Final Note On Bayes' Rule
Final note on
p ( x _ { 0 } , \dots , x _ { T } ) \sim \prod _ { t = 1 } ^ { T } p ( x _ { t } | x _ { t - 1 } , \dots , x _ { 0 } )
If T = 2 the above is p ( x 0 , x 1 , x 2 ) equals p ( x 2 | x 1 , x 0 ) p ( x 1 | x 0 ) and notice that p ( x 0 ) is missing
However, for any sentence x ∈ S we have a term p ( x 0 ) and we can also fix p ( x 0 ); every sentence starts with this padding x 0 = START (or more)
So, we can drop this constant when comparing sentence probabilities
We can just renormalize as needed ...
Trigram Parameter Estimation
Let's fix the Trigram Language Model so that
Note that we have x 0 = x -1 =START since for any x t we have these two words coming before
Note that in this case, we need 2 padding tokens START for each sentence if we apply this to x 1
Training? The goal is to estimate p ( x t | x t -1 , x t -2 ) given some data ... We'll see this in a bit
What about inference? How would we simulate from this model if we had parameters?
Step 1 Set t = 1 and x 0 = x -1 = START Step 2 Generate x t from p ( x t | x t -1 , x t -2 ) Step 3 If x t = STOP, return x 1 , . . . , x t -1 Step 4 Else, set t = t +1 and go to Step 1
Trigram Parameter Estimation
Assume we have the Trigram language model, whose main parameters are θ ( w | v , u )
Assume that w ∈ {V ∪ { STOP }} and u , v ∈ {V ∪ { START }}
The value θ ( w | v , u ) can be interpreted as seeing w after seeing ( u , v )
For any sentence x = ( x 1 , . . . , x T ) we have that
Recall that in the above x 0 = x -1 = START and x T = STOP
Trigram Language Model Example
For example, suppose x = the man STOP and we add two padding tokens x 0 = x -1 =START
The above has probability
θ (the | START , START) θ (man | the , START) θ (STOP | the , man)
The parameters of this model satisfy θ ( w | v , u ) ≥ 0 for any ( u , v , w ) and
The key is to figure out a way to get good estimates for θ ( w | v , u )
w ∈ {V ∪ { STOP }}
u ∈ {V ∪ { START }} and v ∈ {V ∪ { START }}
Notice that θ ( man | START , the) does not make sense; v can be START only if u is

∑ w ∈{V∪{ STOP }} θ ( w | v , u ) = 1
Parameter Estimation and the Trigram Language Model
How do we estimate the terms θ ( w | v , u ) from data?
Assume we have sentences { x ( i ) } N i =1 where each sentence can have a different length T i
Given this, we can form the Log-Likelihood (Loss) function

\mathcal { L } ( \theta ) = - \frac { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \log p ( x ^ { ( i ) } ) } { N } = - \frac { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \log \prod _ { t = 1 } ^ { T _ { i } } \theta ( x _ { t } ^ { ( i ) } | x _ { t - 1 } ^ { ( i ) } , x _ { t - 2 } ^ { ( i ) } ) } { N }
Using log( ab ) = log a +log b , this is
\mathcal { L } ( \theta ) = - \frac { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \sum _ { t = 1 } ^ { T _ { i } } \log \theta ( x _ { t } ^ { ( i ) } | x _ { t - 1 } ^ { ( i ) } , x _ { t - 2 } ^ { ( i ) } ) } { N }
We'd like to minimize L ( θ ) subject to constraints of the form θ ( w | v , u ) ≥ 0 for any ( u , v , w ) and
\sum _ { w \in \{ \mathcal { V } \cup \{ S T O P \} \} } \theta ( w | v , u ) = 1
Parameter Estimation and the Trigram Language Model
Recall that
\mathcal { L } ( \theta ) = - \frac { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \sum _ { t = 1 } ^ { T _ { i } } \log \theta ( x _ { t } ^ { ( i ) } | x _ { t - 1 } ^ { ( i ) } , x _ { t - 2 } ^ { ( i ) } ) } { N }
Before moving on, notice that
\mathcal { L } ( \theta ) = - \frac { \sum _ { ( u , v , w ) } c o u r t ( u , v , w ) \log \theta ( w | v , u ) } { N }
Above, count ( u , v , w ) is the number of times we've seen the Trigram ( u , v , w ) in the corpus
We have some constraints:
In the above u ∈ {V ∪ START }
In the above w ∈ {V ∪ STOP }
If u = START, v ∈ {V ∪ START }
If u ∈ {V} , v ∈ {V}
Trigram Language Model Lagrange Multipliers
A typical way to do this is to introduce Lagrange multipliers λ ( u , v ) and bring the constraints into the objective as

\mathcal { L } ( \theta , \lambda ) = - \frac { \sum _ { ( u , v , w ) } c o u n t ( u , v , w ) \log \theta ( w | v , u ) } { N } + \sum _ { ( u , v ) } \lambda ( u , v ) [ \sum _ { w } \theta ( w | v , u ) - 1 ]
Consider the partial derivative of this with respect to θ ( w | v , u ) for some fixed ( u , v , w )
Let θ ⋆ and λ ⋆ be the optimal parameters
Parameter Estimation and the Trigram Language Model
Thinking about this, we get that this partial derivative is
\frac { \partial \mathcal { L } } { \partial \theta ( w | v , u ) } = - \frac { c o u n t ( u , v , w ) } { N \theta ^ { * } ( w | v , u ) } + \lambda ^ { * } ( u , v ) = 0
This gives θ ⋆ ( w | v , u ) = count ( u , v , w ) N λ ⋆ ( u , v )
Since we have ∑ w ∈{V∪{ STOP }} θ ⋆ ( w | v , u ) = 1
If we sum and use ∑ w count ( u , v , w ) = count ( u , v ) we get
1 = \frac { c o u n t ( u , v ) } { N \lambda ^ { * } ( u , v ) }
This means that count ( u , v ) N = λ ⋆ ( u , v )
Hence, we get ( N cancels) θ ⋆ ( w | v , u ) = count ( u , v , w ) count ( u , v )
Parameter Estimation and the Trigram Language Model
So, the Maximum Likelihood estimates for these parameters are
\theta ^ { * } ( w | v , u ) = \frac { c o u n t ( u , v , w ) } { c o u n t ( u , v ) }
count ( u , v , w ) is the number of times we see this triplet of word tokens in the training corpus (in order)
count ( u , v ) is the number of times we see this pair of word tokens in the training corpus (in order)
Issues? These models are old-school and they had a few
If count ( u , v , w ) is zero, does that mean θ ( w | v , u ) = 0 ... Should this be the case?
If count ( u , v ) is zero, how do we even estimate θ ( w | v , u ) ?

Perplexity: How to measure Language Model Goodness
How do we know if a Language Model is good?
Ideally, it should give a high probability to a good sentence, and a low probability to a bad sentence
We always minimize the Loss L = -∑ N i =1 log p ( x ( i ) ) N , but in NLP we also use another metric called the Perplexity to quantify model goodness
Let T be the sum of all Test sentence lengths and N be the number of Test sentences
Then we have
P e r p l e x i t y = 2 ^ { \frac { N L } { T } }
What is the min / max of Perplexity?
The exponent is of the form -log probability so N L T is between 0 and ∞
A good model has low (Test) Loss, which means the Perplexity is 1 = 2 0
A bad model has a high (Test) Loss, which means the Perplexity is ∞ = 2 ∞
Perplexity For the Uniform Trigram Language Model
Perplexity is a good way to say one Language Model is better than another
What happens if |{V ∪ { STOP }}| = |V| +1 and we set θ ( w | v , u ) = 1 |V| +1 ?
Assume we have sentences { x ( i ) } N i =1 with length T i
We have log p ( x ( i ) ) = log ∏ Ti t =1 θ ( x ( i ) t | x ( i ) t -1 , x ( i ) t -2 )
Define T = ∑ N i =1 T i
Each term log p ( x ( i ) ) = T i log( 1 |V| +1 )
Define Perplexity = 2 N T L where L = -∑ N i =1 log p ( x ( i ) ) N is the loss
Then N T L = -N T ∑ N i =1 Ti N log( 1 |V| +1 ) = log( |V| +1)
So, we have Perplexity is 2 log( |V| +1) = O ( |V| ) for the uniform Trigram Model
The perplexity can be through of as the effective vocabulary size - We do very poorly with a uniform model, we have not compressed the vocabulary in any way ...
Neural Language Models in 2003
What about Neural Network Language Models? We saw one with the gated CNN application
An MLP Language Model? At a high level the way such a model might work is
Fix a convex window n
Get contiguous words from text such that y = ( w t ) and x = ( w t -1 , . . . , w t -n +1 )
For each word w ∈ V , assign a unique word embedding vector C ( w ) of dimension m
Let h be the number of hidden units, m the word embedding dimension
Concatenate the word embeddings for x and set x ∈ R ( n -1) m
Set the parameters b ∈ R |V| , W ∈ R |V|× ( n -1) m , U ∈ R |V|× h , d ∈ R h , H ∈ R h × ( n -1) m and C ∈ R |V|× m
Neural Language Models in 2003
The MLP Language Model is easy to explain if you think of x as the words before the target word y
Set the parameters b ∈ R |V| , W ∈ R |V|× ( n -1) m , U ∈ R |V|× h , d ∈ R h , H ∈ R h × ( n -1) m and C ∈ R |V|× m
Set
y = b + W _ { X } + U * T \tanh ( d + H _ { X } ) \in \mathbb { R } ^ { | V | }
Return softmax ( y ) and as usual compare this probability vector against the ground truth : 'What word goes after ( w t -1 , . . . , w t -n +1 )?'
Notice: the above has skip connections: x → x + f ( x ) so that this allows easier gradient flow
Also notice, the model got the best Perplexity at the time, beating several other popular benchmarks

But, all these basic models had a problem, however: limited (fixed) context
Can we build models with the potential of having unlimited context?
We have via Bayes' rule that
At a high level, any of the CNN/ MLP / N-Gram based models approximates the above, they assume for example that
p ( y _ { t } | y _ { t - 1 } , \dots , y _ { 1 } ) \sim p ( y _ { t } | y _ { t - 1 } , \dots , y _ { t - k } )
The models we look at today do not need such approximations ...

Recurrent Neural Networks: example data

One way get training data for language modeling is to grab chunks of contiguous text x = ( x 0 , x 2 , . . . , x T -1 ) and try to predict the shifted version of this text y = ( x 1 , x 2 , . . . , x T )
For example, you want to map 'START The man walks' to 'The man walks END'
Here x = ( START , ' The ' , ' man ' , ' walks ') and y = (' The ' , ' man ' , ' walks ' , END )
We'd like to have a model that feeds in each x t in sequence and using this x t (and past history) predicts y t
Some notes:
In the example above, the Loss should have 4 terms
Feeding data into this model should be sequential, from left to right
We need a way to pass in new data and carry forward older data
This is Self-Supervised Learning: we took some raw data and built x and y from it out of thin air
Recurrent Neural Networks
In summary we want a model so that we can build
with minimal approximations
Using Bayes' rule, this is equal to
We'd like to NOT approximate this, we'd like to have in some sense a very large or an infinite context
We want models that can truly express terms like
RNN's promise this, but they have some issues as we'll see
Recurrent Neural Networks: Recursions
Each time step of an RNN related h t to h t -1 and x t
At each time step, we pass in x t (the current word)
Additionally, also use the previous h t -1 (a past history)
We have
At each time step, we have ˆ y t = F ( W y h t + b y ) where F is usually a sigmoid or softmax (over |V| possible words)
We initialize h 0 appropriately
At each time step, we have a Loss L t (ˆ y t , y t ) which is usually a Cross-Entropy
The full loss per training pair
pair is an average of the loss across all time steps
\frac { \sum _ { t = 1 } ^ { T } \mathcal { L } _ { t } ( \hat { y } _ { t } , y _ { t } ) } { T }

As the data gets longer, an RNN can no longer propagate dependencies between things far in the future and elements earlier on
Why?
Recurrent Neural Networks: Recursions
Let's do a small example where W h = a , W x = b and b h = c are all scalars and we have the recursion h t = ah t -1 + bx t + c
Assume also we have ˆ y t = dh t and the loss is L t (ˆ y t , y t ) = (ˆ y t -y t ) 2
Here, a , b , c , d are parameters of dimension 1
What is ∂ L 1 ∂ a ?
Here we have ∂ L 1 ∂ a = ∂ L 1 ∂ ˆ y 1 ∂ ˆ y 1 ∂ h 1 ∂ h 1 ∂ a = 2(ˆ y 1 -y 1 ) dh 0
Recurrent Neural Networks: Recursions
Let's do a small example where W h = a , W x = b and b h = c are all scalars and we have the recursion h t = ah t -1 + bx t + c
Assume also we have ˆ y t = dh t and the loss is L t (ˆ y t , y t ) = (ˆ y t -y t ) 2
What is ∂ L 2 ∂ a ?
Here we have ∂ L 2 ∂ a = ∂ L 2 ∂ ˆ y 2 ∂ ˆ y 2 ∂ h 2 ∂ h 2 ∂ a
This is actually ∂ L 2 ∂ ˆ y 2 ∂ ˆ y 2 ∂ h 2 [ ∂ h 2 ∂ a + ∂ h 2 ∂ h 1 ∂ h 1 ∂ a ]
This is 2(ˆ y 2 -y 2 ) d ( h 1 + ah 0 )
Note the appearance of the a term
Recurrent Neural Networks: Recursions
What is ∂ L 4 ∂ a ?
Here we have ∂ L 4 ∂ a = ∂ L 4 ∂ ˆ y 4 ∂ ˆ y 4 ∂ h 4 ∂ h 4 ∂ a
This is actually

\frac { \partial \mathcal { L } _ { 4 } } { \partial a } = \sum _ { t = 1 } ^ { 3 } \frac { \partial \mathcal { L } _ { 4 } } { \partial \hat { y } _ { 4 } } \frac { \partial \hat { y } _ { 4 } } { \partial h _ { 4 } } \frac { \partial h _ { 4 } } { \partial h _ { t } } \frac { \partial h _ { t } } { \partial a } + \frac { \partial \mathcal { L } _ { 4 } } { \partial \hat { y } _ { 4 } } \frac { \partial \hat { y } _ { 4 } } { \partial h _ { 4 } } \frac { \partial h _ { 4 } } { \partial a }
Simplified, this is
What happens when a is small or large?
Recurrent Neural Networks: Gradients Issues
We have the gradient ∂ L 4 was
∂ a 2(ˆ y 4 -y 4 ) da 3 h 0 +2(ˆ y 4 -y 4 ) da 2 h 1 +2(ˆ y 4 -y 4 ) dah 2 +2(ˆ y 4 -y 4 ) dh 3
If a is small, we have that the term measuring the dependency between the current time step and the one in the past is less and less
This is called the vanishing gradient problem
If a is too large, we'll have an infinite gradient, another problem
This is called the exploding gradient problem
Recurrent Neural Networks: Gradients Issues
You can also get the recursion over and over and see what happens
You can also see this (do the recursion over and over):
If h t is going to predict y t , we clearly see old information 'vanishes' if a is small
In some sense this illustrates the problem
Recurrent Neural Networks: Solving Gradient Issues
The exploding gradient problem can be solved by gradient clipping
The vanishing gradient problem might be solved by other ways
Residual connections
Xavier Initialization (some sort of smart initialization)
Momentum based optimizers (Adam)
None of the above are principled, they 'might' work
In practice, vanilla RNNs do not perform as good as they theoretically can due to the vanishing gradient problem
More complicated (LSTM, Transformers, Attention) models are used
Recurrent Neural Networks: Truncated BP
Doing back propagation in RNN models can be expensive since you need to go all the way back to t = 0
We generally have

\frac { \partial \mathcal { L } _ { t } } { \partial a } = \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { t } \frac { \partial \mathcal { L } _ { t } } { \partial \hat { y } _ { t } } \frac { \partial \hat { y } _ { t } } { \partial h _ { t } } \frac { \partial h _ { t } } { \partial h _ { s } } \frac { \partial h _ { s } } { \partial a }
I use ∂ ht ∂ ht = 1 for the case s = t
One idea is to simplify the above to be

\frac { \partial \mathcal { L } _ { t } } { \partial a } = \sum _ { s = t - k } ^ { t } \frac { \partial \mathcal { L } _ { t } } { \partial \hat { y } _ { t } } \frac { \partial \hat { y } _ { t } } { \partial h _ { t } } \frac { \partial h _ { t } } { \partial h _ { s } } \frac { \partial h _ { s } } { \partial a }
Thus, for each term in the loss L t , we only look back a fixed window when computing the gradients
This works somewhat, but now we are back to the finite context case

Recurrent Neural Networks: Bidirectional RNN
You can also have models which have context on both sides
Imagine the sentiment analysis of 'The great movie was actually really just expensive and bad.'
You'd like to know that 'great' has a bit of irony in it and it contrasts with 'expensive and bad'
Sentiment analysis is one example where a bidirectional RNN might be a good idea: each word has the best context of those in the sentence!
'a picture" or "
a model.
Recurrent Neural Networks: Bidirectional RNN
When you are generating text, you can't use a truly bidirectional RNN (given our architecture) since then you are cheating
For that, you'd need to run a model left-to-right and another (independent) model right-to-left and then fuse the results to get (weak) bidirectionally
Imagine 'He painted the wall with a brush, not a spray can.'
x = ( START , he , painted , . . . , a , spray , can )
y = ( he , painted , . . . , not , a , spray , can , END )
A left-to-right RNN might initially struggle to determine what 'painted the' refers to until it reaches 'wall'.

A bidirectional RNN, however, can look ahead and see 'with a brush' while also considering 'painted the', making it easier to correctly infer that 'wall' is the object being painted, rather than, say, 'a picture' or 'a model'.

LSTM
RNN suffer from vanishing gradient problems
Because of this, people have tried to come up with variants that resolve this issue
LSTM (1997 !) are one of the most popular variants
Empirically, an LSTM has been shown to have a memory of about 100 time steps, whereas a RNN has about just 7
LSTM
Dynamics of the LSTM are as follows
We still have data x = ( x 0 , x 1 , . . . , x T ) but now we have a more complicated interaction
Specifically, each time step not only has h t , but now there is a cell state c t that is carried forward
We have
Where the state is
c _ { t } = \sigma ( f _ { t } ) \odot c _ { t - 1 } + \sigma ( i _ { t } ) \odot \tanh ( g _ { t } )
And the hidden state is
h _ { t } = \sigma ( o _ { t } ) \odot \tanh ( c _ { t } )
LSTM
The key new feature here is
c _ { t } = \sigma ( f _ { t } ) \odot c _ { t - 1 } + \sigma ( i _ { t } ) \odot \tanh ( g _ { t } )
σ ( f t ) is the forget gate
σ ( i t ) is the update gate
This cell state is a combination of old information and new information: the sole purpose of this state is to help the network not forget!
And the hidden state is h t = σ ( o t ) ⊙ tanh ( c t ) is a function of new information and the new/old information in the cell state
As we will show, this affects the gradients: there is less chance of vanishing gradient phenomenon and a current loss update can use information from far out in the past!
LSTM: Parameters
In the above
W h ∈ R dh × 4 dh
W x ∈ R dx × 4 dh
b ∈ R 4 dh
h 0 , c 0 ∈ R dh
σ is the sigmoid
⊙ is the Hadamard product, [ A ⊙ B ] ij = A ij B ij
LSTM: Parameters
So we can be more specific with the parameters
Note that in reality we can think of W x = [ W fx , W ix , W ox , W gx ]

GRU: Simpler LSTM
Simplification of LSTM: GRU
A GRU defines reset and update gate logits via
For a GRU, we have g t = W gh ( σ ( r t ) h t -1 ) + W gx x t + b g
Now, h t = σ ( z t ) ⊙ tanh ( g t ) + (1 -σ ( z t )) ⊙ h t -1

The reset gate σ ( r t ) allows the GRU to use or ignore the previous state when computing the next candidate g t
GRU models offer less parameters than a LSTM and have been shown to do about as well

Are LSTMs dead now?
Sort of, but there are some ideas which may bring them back
Why are they dead?
Main problem: parallelism
If I give you a batch of sentences, to compute this batch you need to go one by one though each word in each sentence to get the hidden states
You can parallelize across difference sentences, but not within each sentence
GPU cannot be used as well as possible

Decoding: Example
Once we train a model, we have the problem of decoding: How do we generate text using the model?
Usually, we start with a context like 'The man walks' or a special start token ' START ' and begin decoding with the model
One very simple variant: greedy decoding
This is one of the oldest and easiest heuristics
Decoding: Greedy Search Example
Basic idea: Always pick the highest probability next word and use that as your next word
Once picked, add the word to your context and repeat until some terminal condition happens
Big idea is that, for example, we have
p ( The , man , walks , y ) = p ( y | The , man , walks ) p ( The , man , walks )
We can just focus on
p ( y | The , man , walks )
if we want to maximize p ( The , man , walks , y )
Decoding: Greedy Search Example
So suppose the context is ' The man walks '
Get
argmax y p ( y | The , man , walks )
and suppose this is ' down '
Note that technically we don't work with strings, but the integers that each string represent
Now the context is 'The man walks down'
Get
argmax y p ( y | The , man , walks , down )
and suppose this is ' the '
Now the context is ' the '
Etc. Repeat this until the context is done or until a special end ' END ' token is generated
Once picked, add the word to your context and repeat over and over

Greedy, generally, will not give the optimal solution - we keep just one choice!
Also, human generated text is not generated this way: you don't always pick the word with the next highest probability to generate the things you say
Sometimes, you make a lower probability choice but in the long run this leads to higher probability choices overall
Think of generating this word by word: 'The Himalayan Mountains are very nice'
Another idea: Beam Search
Decoding: Beam Search
Instead of picking the next highest probability word to add to your context, store a beam of size k as your set of candidates
Example: Assume k = 2 and your context is again ' The man walks '
When we look at

we don't just pick y = ' down ', we also pick ' fast ', which has the second highest probability
Now you have two context, ' The man walks down ' and ' The man walks fast '
Next time, consider
p ( y | The , man , walks , down ) p ( The , man , walks , down ) and p ( y | The , man , walks , fast ) p ( The , man , walks , fast )
and get the k = 2 maximum probability tokens

NMT
Sometimes we want to go from one input sequence to another output sequence
These are known as Seq2Seq problems
Some examples:
Machine Translation: Go from one language to another language
Summarization: Go from a large piece of text to a smaller piece of text
Chatbots: Generate a piece of text which is an answer to some input question
Code Generation: Words to Python code
Named Entity Recognition: Sentence to Tags
Parsing: Sentence to output parse
We'll focus on Translation here
Neural MT vs Statistical MT
It would be good to know how this was done before by looking at SMT = Statistical Machine Translation
Suppose we want to translate from x (French) to y (English)
Let x have length T x and y have length T y
From 1990 - 2010, people focused on modeling p ( y | x )
If you look at this a bit, you'll notice that (Bayes' Rule)
\arg \max _ { y } p ( y | x ) = \arg \max _ { y } p ( x | y ) p ( y )
This is 2 different problems
Problem 1: Model p ( x | y ) (Translation)
Problem 2: Model p ( y ) (Language Model)
SMT
Problem 2: Model p ( y ) (Language Model)
Learning p ( y ) can be done via for example n gram models or otherwise - we saw this
This model deals with good fluency; how do we write proper English?
Problem 1: Model p ( x | y ) (Translation)
Learning p ( x | y ) can be done if we have data ( x , y ) which we know has y mapping to x
This model deals with good fidelity: how should words and phrases be translated?
This model cares more about getting the right words across, not making sense
At a high level, if we solve both Problem 1 and Problem 2 well, the y that we pick needs to be decent both for p ( x | y ) and p ( y ), so it should sense and contain the right words
SMT Data
We have parallel data from many sources
For example, human translations (Books, TV, Court Proceedings)
One of the oldest examples is the Rosetta Stone
Using Data for SMT
How do we use this data?
To build translation models, people usually needed phrase translation tables; phrases were built by first getting alignments so alignments were the first step
To get alignments, what people typically did was they considered models that had structure on p ( x , a | y ), where
p ( x | y ) = \sum _ { a } p ( x , a | y )
Here, ' a ' was usually labeled ' alignment ' and it was a latent variable that told us for each target ( x ) word which source ( y ) word generated it
Put another way: an alignment is, for each word in x , a word in y that was used to generate
Note: some models handled the case when for a certain word x t there was no word y at which generated it

What is Neural Machine Translation?
At a high level, NMT is doing machine translation but using a single network
Let's spell out the picture above a bit more
Here, x is the input, y is the output; T x , T y are the number of tokens in each
NMT was a small effort in 2014 - it blew up!

input sentence in reverse, because doing so introduces many short term dependencies in the data that make the optimization problem much easier.
▶ 2 Models: An encoder and a decoder
Encoder and decoder were usually 2 separate RNN models (no parameter sharing)
Encoder RNN can have a different structure than the decoder RNN (encoder = LSTM, decoder = special GRU (?))
The representation of the input would usually be the final encoder hidden states h Tx
h Tx = k 0 , the final encoder hidden layer states are the initial decoder hidden layer states

The input is fed in and we get the final hidden values per layer
These final hidden values initialize the corresponding hidden layers in the decoder
We train by Teacher Forcing on the decoder side: we aim to predict ( y 1 , y 2 , . . . , y Ty +1 ) from ( y 0 , y 1 , . . . , y Ty )
Note y 0 = < s > and y Ty +1 = < / s > are the special start of sentence and end of sentence tokens
Specifically, it is like we have a language model to train but we initialize it with a special set of values ( h Tx )
NMT: Layers
To get the best performance, we can use a (bidirectional) stacked LSTM for the encoder
The decoder RNN is autoregressive: it can't look into the future (it's a sort of language model!)
We can use a stacked (one directional) LSTM for the decoder
The idea is that lower layers compute general features, while higher layers compute more complex interactions: both are crucial
Seems like 2-4 layers is about the sweet spot in the number of layers needed
2 layers better than 1
3 layers a little better than 2
The more layers you have, the more you'll need connections, tricks, etc
NMT vs SMT - Advantages
Compared to SMT, NMT has many advantages
Better performance
More fluent
Better use of context
Better use of phrase similarities
1 Neural Network, not 12 models in a disjoint 'pipeline'
Much less human engineering effort
NMT vs SMT - Disadvantages
Disadvantages of NMT?
NMT is less interpretable and hard to debug
NMT is difficult to control: we can't really put in rules and and get the result we want
BLEU - How do we say a translation is 'good'?
How do we measure the quality of a translation?
Source: Le chat est sur le tapis
MT System Output: the the the the the the the
Reference 1: The cat is on the mat
Reference 2: There is a cat on the mat
How do we know this is a bad translation?
One idea is to measure the precision of the translation
For a general MT output we can get the precision by
Getting all the words (tokens) in the generated translation
Counting if each word is or is not in the reference translations
Dividing by the number of words in the generated translation
For the example above we have 1 word 7 times
'the' is seen in at least one of the reference sentences
Reminder: Precision = | hits | | predictions |
For us, we get Precision = 7 7
But this is bad, we clearly have a bad translation
BLEU
One possible idea is to clip the maximum count for each token in the generated output by the maximum count in any of the references
For example, the count of 'the' can't be more than 2 in either of the reference sentences, so we'll clip this word's count
Specifically we define

\text {Precision} = \frac { \sum _ { w \in \hat { y } } \text {Count} _ { c l i p } ( w ) } { \sum _ { w \in \hat { y } } \text {Count} ( w ) } = \frac { \sum _ { w \in \hat { y } } \text {Count} _ { c l i p } ( w ) } { | \hat { y } | }
▶ Specifically, this is
For each unique word w in the generated translation
Get Count ( w ) , the number of times the word w is in the generated output
Get Count clip ( w ), the minimum of Count ( w ) and the maximum number of times w is in some reference sentence ∑
Return w ∈ ˆ y Countclip ( w ) ∑ w ∈ ˆ y Count ( w )
With this definition, we get a Precision of 2 / 7 since 'the' is in the generated translation 7 times and in the first reference 2 times
BLEU
So, clipping solved one problem
But, what if we have
Translation 1: The cat is on the mat
Translation 2 (Yoda): Cat on the mat it is
Clearly, Translation 1 is better but we would not pick this up if we just use tokens w that are unigrams (words)
To this end, we can use bigrams, trigrams, and 4-grams to capture word order
For example, for Translation 1, we have bigrams 'the cat', 'cat is', 'is on', 'on the', 'the mat'
We can then do the same exact thing as before, but this time use w as a bigram instead
BLEU
The precisions we calculate for 1, 2, 3 and 4 grams are p i
Then, BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy) is
Another problem: short translations can boost BLEU scores; how can we prevent this?
Why do short translations boost BLEU?
Reminder: Precision = | hits | | predictions |
Introduce a Brevity penalty
Typically this looks like min r penalty ( c , r ) where c refers to a candidate and r refers to a reference
Here, penalty ( c , r ) = e 1 -| r | / | c | if | c | ≤ | r | and otherwise 1
The idea is to make sure generated translation c is about the same length (or longer) as the references

Interpretation
Almost useless
Hard to get the gist
The ranges of BLEU indicate empirically how good the translation is
There might be better metrics, for example 'GLEU', ''RIBES', 'BEER', etc
BLEU is still the most standard metric used today
Very high quality, adequate, and fluent translations
Quality often better than human
BLEU - More on Positives and Negatives
▶ Positives
It is quick to calculate and corresponds with the way a human would evaluate the same text
It is used very widely, which makes it easier to compare your results with other work
Importantly, it is language-independent making it straightforward to apply to your NLP models
It can be used when you have more than one ground truth sentence
▶ Negatives

It does not consider the meaning of words. It is perfectly acceptable to a human to use a different word with the same meaning eg. Use 'watchman' instead of 'guard'. But BLEU Score considers that an incorrect word
It looks only for exact word matches. Sometimes a variant of the same word can be used eg. 'rain' and 'raining', but BLEU Score counts that as an error

It ignores the importance of words. With BLEU Score an incorrect word like 'to' or 'an' that is less relevant to the sentence is penalized just as heavily as a word that contributes significantly to the meaning of the sentence

It does not consider the order of words eg. The sentence 'The guard arrived late because of the rain' and 'The rain arrived late because of the guard' would get the same (unigram) BLEU Score even though the latter is quite different
Luty, the Dueu scores went lo about su (or mgner)
BLEU and NMT
In 2014, there was a fringe attempt to use NMT
By 2016, NMT took over - Google switched to NMT
SMT essentially died out as its performance had somewhat plateaued 10
By 2019, the BLEU scores went to about 50 (or higher)
I phrase-based SMT
syntax-based SMT
neural MT
NMT Issues
Currently, problems in NMT focus on still to be solved critical problems
Low resource languages (what do we do if we have no data?)
Domain mismatch between train and test data
Out of vocabulary words
Bias pick up (gender neutral sentences become gender specific)
Maintaining context over longer pairs (we'll see a valuable tool for this next)

Attention: Motivation
For a general NMT problem we saw that we feed in ( x 1 , . . . , x Tx ) and expect to get ( y 1 , . . . , y Ty )
Assume we use an encoder GRU over x and that the final hidden state is h Tx
This final state becomes the initial hidden state of the decoder over y , h Tx = k 0
One big problem with this approach is k 0 now has to carry all the information from the input x
This was anticipated: to get better results, the authors of the original NMT model fed x in reverse order so that y t and x t are 'close'
Attention: Motivation
Imagine we have a sentence x = 'I went to the flower market to buy some flowers'
Imagine the translation is y = 'Ich ging zum Blumenmarkt, um Blumen zu kaufen '
If we compress everything into h Tx , the resulting vector needs to contain
Information about the subject ('I')
Information about the verbs ('buy' and 'went')
Information about the objects ('flowers' and 'flower market')
Interaction of the subjects, verbs, and objects with each other in the sentence
This is a lot of information!
Attention

Attention: Benefits
Attention improves NMT performance
It is useful to allow the decoder to focus on certain parts of the source
Attention solves the bottleneck problem
Attention allows the decoder to look directly at the source; we bypass the bottleneck h Tx
Attention helps with vanishing gradient problems
We have provided the model with shortcuts to distant states
Attention offers some interpretability
By inspecting the attention distribution, we can see what the decoder was focusing on

Attention
Attention is a general idea
The Transformer which we'll see soon is based on this idea, but the attention defined there is a bit different (slightly different than this, but at a high level the same thing)
Attention has a few other variants
Recall we defined e ts = v ⊺ a tanh ( W a k t -1 + U a h s ) and then α = Softmax ( e )
How can we change this?
Attention: Language Models
We can use Attention for language models
Recall for Language Modeling we have ( y 0 , . . . , y T -1 ) predicting ( y 1 , . . . , y T )
Typically, we have a recursion like h t = f ( y t , h t -1 ) and then predict ˆ y t ∼ y t +1 from h t
To augment this, we can create α ts = g ( h t , h s ) for s < t
We can then take a softmax on α and set
c _ { t } = \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { t } \alpha _ { t s } h _ { s }
and predict using c t
Crucially, note that we do not look forward, but just in the past
Attention: Global Variants
Many variants
e ts = score ( k t -1 , h s ) or e ts = score ( k t , h s )
In the first variant, we use e ts = score ( k t -1 , h s ) to get c t and improve k t and predict y t +1 from k t = f ( y t , k t -1 , c t )
In the second variant, we use e ts = score ( k t , h s ) to get c t and predict y t from [ k t , c t ]
Here k , h are respectively the target and source hidden states
Upshot: We can make make α ts depend on k t -1 or k t
There are many options for score
score ( k t , h s ) = k ⊺ t h s (dot)
score ( k t , h s ) = k ⊺ t W a h s (general)
score ( k t , h s ) = v ⊺ a tanh ( W a k t + U a h s ) (concat)
score ( k t , h s ) = W a k t (location)
This can be called 'Global' attention since we always look at the FULL encoder history
Attention: Global Variants
Global attention focuses on the entire input

Attention: Local Variants
One problem with the Global attention above is that perhaps we will have too many computations for very long source sentences (documents)
We can define 'Local' attention which aims to focus on a specific subset of source sentence words
To get this working, we can set D to a constant and then define for each target position t an index p t in the source sentence
To compute the context vector c t , we just focus on an interval [ p t -D , p t + D ] of source words
p t = T x ∗ ( t / T y ) (local-m)
p t = T x σ ( v ⊺ p tanh ( W p k t )) (local-p)

ELMo: LSTM details
After optimization, if each LSTM has L layers, we have representations at each time step t
← -h 0 t , . . . , ← -h L t for the backward LSTM
- → h 0 t , . . . , - → h L t for the forward LSTM
We can get a layer level representation by setting h l t = ( - → h l t , ← -h l t )
Also: h 0 t is the initial embeddings from the model, which use character level convolutions to learn word embeddings
ELMo: How to get the embeddings
Given a token x t and a specific task, ELMo uses a combination of all h l t to represent x t
We push the data through the trained LSTM, and get h 0 t , . . . , h L t
For a specific task, we then use ElMo ( x t ) = γ task ∑ L l =0 s task l h l t
Note that we do not just take the final layer! We combine layers!
γ task is a parameter that is task specific
( s task 0 , . . . , s task L ) are parameters whose values is conditioned to be positive and sum to 1
For a general RNN model, you can now pass ( Word 2 Vec ( x t ) , ELMo ( x t )) to the RNN instead of just Word 2 Vec ( x t )
For SQuAD or SNLI (Entailment) tasks they use ELMo at the top of the output RNN, so you use ( k t , ELMo ( x t )) to predict, not just k t

Fine tuning: Spelled out
We use the above term often but I just want to spell it out before moving forward
The way this works is we have a loss L 1 ( θ ) and we consider min θ L 1 ( θ )
For example, the above L 1 might be a Cross-Entropy loss if we do language modeling
Once the above is done we append some new parameters ϑ and consider another loss L 2 ( θ, ϑ ); the θ parameters are initialized with the θ we got from the L 1 optimization
For example, min θ,ϑ L 2 ( θ, ϑ ) might correspond to some text classification objective where the input to the classifier is the average of all final token embeddings
The classifier 'head' is just a MLP and has a small relative number of parameters
Big Idea 1: Optimizing L 2 is hard, but initializing with the parameters from L 1 gives a big boost
Big Idea 2: The general features learning via L 1 are extremely powerful

The Transformer: Why?
What's wrong with RNN models given what we saw before?
Main issue: people started observing that you can train large models and then fine tune them to different tasks
Think ULM-Fit: train an LSTM and then fine-tune
Or ELMo: train an LSTM and then for each token get a mixture of the hidden layer representations,
E M o ( x _ { t } ) = \gamma ^ { t a s k } \sum _ { l = 0 } ^ { L } s _ { l } ^ { t a s k } h _ { t } ^ { l } ,
where
You optimize for γ task and { s task l } L l =0
You might fine-tune the entire of ELMo ( x t ) as well ( h L t )
Problem: Generally, bigger models were seen to do better, but RNNs can't be parallelized!
Why? For each element in a batch, you need to traverse its full length T sequentially, you cannot parallelize
pardlelize across milers
The Transformer: We Want Parallelism
In an RNN, for a sequence { x 1 , . . . , x T } we need to process ( h 0 , x 1 ), then ( h 1 , x 2 ), then ( h 2 , x 3 ), Etc.
Contrast this with a CNN: if we run a filter over the data, we can parallelize the application of that filter to different chunks of the data
Moreover, we can also parallelize across filters
The transformer replaced the RNN/LSTM idea with an Attention based substitute that allows for parallelism
The effective batch size of a Transformer/CNN is the number of words
Contrast this with an LSTM: effective batch size is the number of sequences
What is Self-Attention?
Big idea of the Transformer: Self-Attention
We have a set of entities
Each of these has a vector representation
What do we want? We want to re-express the vector representations so that all entities know about all other entities
What is Self-Attention?
Big idea of the Transformer: Self-Attention
We have a particular entity v (a vector representation) and we have entity representations { v 1 , . . . , v T } for all the entities
We could represent v in terms of { v 1 , . . . , v T } directly ...
Maybe: v ∼ ∑ T t =1 sim ( v , v t ) ∗ v t

But imagine a database analogy to do more instead ... We are generalizing the above, because maybe we should use different vectors for our source, for the mixing strength, and for the values ... So ...
Assume you have a query q , a vector
Assume you have a set of key vectors { k 1 , . . . , k T }
Assume each key has a value vector representation { v 1 , . . . , v T }
Given this, how can we express q in terms of v using the similarity between q and k ?
Idea: Attention!
Self-Attention
For each key { k t } T t =1 you have a score score t = q ⊺ k t
For all the scores, you can apply the softmax to get alignment probabilities a = softmax ( score ) so a is a vector of probabilities
Then, we can represent q as ∑ T t =1 a t v t , a weighted sum of vectors
Assume that q , k , v ∈ R d and their components are i.i.d. N (0 , 1) random variables ... What is the mean and variance of q ⊺ k ?
We have E ( q j ) = 0 and Var ( q j ) = 1 and similarly for k j
E ( q ⊺ k ) = ∑ d j =1 E ( q j ) E ( k j ) = 0
Var ( q ⊺ k ) = Var ( ∑ d j =1 q j k j ) = ∑ d j =1 Var ( q j k j ) = ∑ d j =1 Var ( q j ) Var ( k j ) = d
Because of this, to fix the scaling, we set score t = q ⊺ k t / √ d since then score t will now have mean 0 and variance 1
Transformer Idea and NLP Data
We have source language ( x 1 , . . . , x Tx ) and target language ( y 0 , y 1 , . . . , y Ty )
Given sentence x , we want to learn to generate sentence y , the translation
Example: x = ('the' 'dog') and y = (START, le, chien, STOP)
To train, the inputs are ( x 1 , . . . , x Tx ) and ( y 0 , . . . , y Ty -1 ) and y 0 is a special start token
To train, the targets are ( y 1 , . . . , y Ty ) and y Ty might be a special stop token
Each x t , y t maps to an embedding, and we change these embeddings
We will
Use self attention to get vector representations of x t that take into account all other words x s
Use self attention to get vector representations of y t that that into account all other words { x t } and y s with s < t
Predict as usual the targets ( y 1 , . . . , y Ty )
Crucially, the above will be amenable to lots of parallelism
Transformer Idea and Data
Crucially, the above will be amenable to lots of parallelism
More on Self-Attention in NLP
At a high level, the mechanism above is called Self-Attention: you have a query q and you use its similarity with some keys { k t } T t =1 to get a expression for q in terms of { v t } T t =1
Example: for NLP, we have a sequence of token embeddings { x 1 , . . . , x T } ... How can we use the above?
Assume each token embedding is dimension d model
Well, what if we fix W Q ∈ R dmodel × dq , W K ∈ R dmodel × dk and W V ∈ R dmodel × dv as matrices
Create q t = W Q x t , the query vectors
Create k t = W K x t , the key vectors
Create v t = W V x t , the value vectors
Since we will take inner products of q t and { k s } T s =1 , d q = d k
For each q t , we can get a set of weights a ts = softmax ( q ⊺ t ks √ dk ) and get a new representation
\sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { T } a _ { t s } v _ { s }
Attention with Matrices
If you have T queries-key-values we can succinctly write the new representations is SoftMax
A t t e n t i o n ( Q , K , V ) = s o f t \max ( \frac { Q K ^ { \top } } { \sqrt { d _ { k } } } ) V
Notice: Across all tokens, you can get the new representations very efficiently!
Q ∈ R T × dk
K ∈ R T × dk
V ∈ R T × dv ; usually d v = d k
In the NLP example, we went from x → q , k , v via W Q , K , V and then applied Attention; Q = XW Q , K = XW K , V = XW V
Multi-Head Attention
The discussion above can be generalized to Multi-Head Attention: you have h different triplet { W Q , K , V i } h i =1 and do the same thing
head i = Attention ( XW Q i , XW K i , XW V i )
You can do this h times
You can start with X and go to Q i , K i , V i via W Q , K , V i
For each { Q i , K i , V i } h i =1 you apply the Self-Attention
After applying the logic above with multiple heads, you concatenate the results to get something of dimension hd v
Finally, we can have a matrix W O ∈ R hdv × dmodel which projects back to d model space directly
We then have
Multihead ( Q , K , V ) = Concat ( head 1 , head 2 , . . . , head h ) W O
Idea: each triple head { W Q , K , V } h i =1 can learn different things so each head i is different
is (nead1,...,
Multi-head Attention: Spelled-out Example!
Suppose you have h = 8 heads
Suppose you have a sentence { x 1 , . . . , x T } with word embeddings per token and d model = 512 and X ∈ R T × 512
We have d k = d q = d model / 8 = 64
Suppose we have 8 sets of triplets { W Q , K , V i } 8 i =1
We map X → Q i ∈ R T × 64 via Q i = XW Q i and similarly for K i , V i ; { W Q , K , V i } h i =1 ∈ R 512 × 64
Apply Self-Attention to get the new head i representations
Each head i ∈ R T × 64 and we concatenate to get ( head 1 , . . . , head 8 ) = R T × 512
The final representation is ( head 1 , . . . , head 8 ) W O
Review of Attention
Starting with initial embeddings x t , this mechanism allows us
Get different representations of q t , k t and v t
Using the association between q t and k s , get a new representation of q t that's aware of all the other k s
Since we start with x t , this is like a contextual representation of x t in terms of all other x s , but in another space
Multi-Head Attention: Why do we have 1 set of matrices W Q , K , V ; why not have h of them and then concatenate the results?
So, for each x t we get a new representation of total length d model → hd v
We map back to dimension d model via W O
Typically, d v = d k and we can pick h , d k so that d model = hd k
oapel.
Back to the Transformer
Translation
The original Transformer was an Encoder-Decoder model
The main use case was Machine Translation, where we have a source sentence and want to translate to a target sentence Vectors
From the COVE paper:
But some differences
Encoder is no longer LSTM, it's a model based on Self-Attention
Word Vectors are not used, instead WordPiece tokenization is used (see later)
Transformer Encoder: Position Embeddings
At a high level, we want to mix the representations x via Self-Attention so that a position x t is aware of the ones around it
This way, we can build a representation that's context-aware
If you look at Attention above, you'll notice that it is position invariant! We need to add in positional information
'a b c d' and 'd c a b' give the same head representation
To disallow this, we add positional embeddings so that we use x t + p t where p t tells us we are at the start, middle, etc of the sentence
To help with gradients, we add residual connections and layer norm (LN)

After each Self-Attention application, we pass each embedding through a Feed-Forward Neural Network with a ReLU activation that projects each token's embedding up to 4 ∗ d model dimension and then back down to d model space
We repeat this process several times until we get a good enough representation
esentations, a n
Transformer Encoder - Algorithm of Encoder
Step 1: X is the initial embeddings matrix and P 1 is the positional encoding matrix
Step 2: Set X = X + P 1
Step 3: Pick a number of heads h and do Multi-Head Self-Attention for each triplet { W Q , K , V 1 , i } h i =1 and then concatenate to get H 1 Inputs
Step 4: Project H 1 so H 1 = H 1 W O 1
Step 5: Set X = LayerNorm ( X + H 1 )

This can easily be done by using a triangular mask over the score matrix so that score ts = -∞ if s > t

What is WordPiece?
The authors used WordPiece which is a tokenization that expressed words like 'immaculate' as 2 tokens 'imma' + '##culate'
'immaculate' → 'imma' + '##culate'
The tokes you choose have an impact on your model ...
What is wrong with just using words and characters?
Characters will almost surely cover all words (no '[UNK]')
But characters lose semantic meaning
Also, 4 Words could map into 20 characters so then you have to keep a long context and have models which are good at memory
On the other hand, words require huge vocabularies and also what do you do when you have a new word?
Instead of the most granular (characters) or the widest (words), find a better middle ground
What is WordPiece?
High level idea of WordPiece:
Step 1: Begin with a set of character tokens
Step 2: Break up all words into tokens and get scores for tokens being next to each other
Step 3: From the most popular tokens next to each other, make a new token which is the concatenation of the above
Step 4: Given the new token, get a count as in Step 2 by greedily breaking up words into tokens
Step 5: Repeat Step 2 - Step 4 until you get a token universe of the desired size or you can't go on
The transformer used 32000 tokens as its vocabulary V
sume the Corpus is a bunch of words with trequencies
WordPiece: Learning tokens
Assume the Corpus is a bunch of words with frequencies
Tokenize each at the most granular level
For example, 'abc' gets tokenized to 'a', '##b', '##c' since 'b', 'c' are inside of a word
The above leads to each token having counts equal to:
Given the above, the vocabulary is
For any pair of tokens, define the 'information' score as
Find the pair with the largest score, and create a new token by merging the tokens t 1 and t 2 ; consider the new data
Repeat until you can't merge anymore or the vocabulary size is large enough
Word-Piece: Decoding
With WordPiece, a word gets tokenized from left to right greedily; we try and use the largest token in the vocabulary as we go from left to right
If we get to a place where a subword is not in the vocabulary, we classify the whole word as '[UNK]'
For example
'bugs'
'hug'
'bum'
→ ['UNK'], since '##um' is not in the vocabulary

GPT1: Bype Pair Encoding
GPT 1 used Byte-Pair encoding to get the universe of tokens
This is very similar to WordPiece but a bit different
You get all words and then start with all unique characters
As in WordPiece, you define a score score ( t 1 , t 2 ) = freq ( t 1 , t 2 )
Identify the most frequent pair and add the rule ( t 1 , t 2 ) → t 1 t 2
Keep track of the order of the rules and use these rules when tokenizing new words

How much do you mask?
Authors proposed masking 15% of all tokens
Of these, 80% become mask in the training data
10% remain as is but have a loss term
10% get a random word inserted on the x side and the loss needs to recover the right word
The authors figured that doing the above would force the model to be on its toes: it needs to get good representations for everything not just tokens which have '[MLM]'
At test time, '[MLM]' is not used, so the above tries to make training ∼ test

Fine Tuning: Start with LM →{ NER, Classification }
Recall the idea of fine tuning
We want to solve some Task 1 (Classification, NER, MT)
To do this, we first pick another Task 2 (LM)
We optimize min θ L LM ( θ )
We take the parameters ˆ θ LM and use it to seed another optimization whose goal is to solve Task 1
We optimize min θ L Classification ( θ )
Typically, Task 2 seeds the word embeddings or features fed into Task 1
I.e. L Classification is fed features by inserting data through the model gotten from optimizing L LM
Option 1: Task 2 as a feature extractor: do not back propagate gradients into Task 1's architecture, leave it static
Option 2: Task 2 fine tuning: back propagate gradients both into the new task specific 'head' AND Task 2's model

Knowledge Distillation
Suppose we have a large model, which performs really well
What are some problems?
Maybe it is very hard to train this model
Maybe the inference speed is prohibitively large
Maybe you need the model on a phone ('Edge Device'), and you don't have memory
Knowledge Distillation offers one way to addresses this issue: you use the larger (teacher) model to help a smaller (student) model learn
Let's look at the case where the task is Classification with C classes
Consider one example with C classes and let { v j } C j =1 and { z j } C j =1 be the logits (pre-softmax) of the larger (teacher) and smaller (student) model
Let T > 0 be a chosen fixed and consider
p ( T ) = \text {softmax} ( v / T ) \text { and } q ( T ) = \text {softmax} ( z / T )
q = softmax ( z ) as usual, no T
Knowledge Distillation
What is the effect of T on p ( T ) = softmax ( v / T )
T = 1, do nothing
T → 0, this converges to one hot vector, which is 1 at the index where v is maximum
T →∞ , this converges to the uniform probability over C
Example: Suppose we want to predict digits 1 - 10, p 7 = 0 . 8 and p 1 = 0 . 1, p i ∼ 0 for i = 7 , 1
If we pick T → 0 then p 7 ( T ) , p 1 ( T ) go up and the other levels become even more residual
Effectively, you might want the student to get information from 1 , 7, but not from other levels, and T allows this
After you train the teacher, you pick α > 0 and minimize:
- \alpha \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { C } y _ { j } \log q _ { j } - ( 1 - \alpha ) T ^ { 2 } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { C } p ( T ) _ { j } \log q ( T ) _ { j }
α , T , p ( T ), y are constants here
Knowledge Distillation
After you train the teacher, you pick α > 0 and minimize:
- \alpha \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { C } y _ { j } \log q _ { j } - ( 1 - \alpha ) T ^ { 2 } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { C } p ( T ) _ { j } \log q ( T ) _ { j }
The above is
\alpha C E ( y , q _ { S t u d e n t } ) + ( 1 - \alpha ) T ^ { 2 } C E ( p _ { T e a c h e r ( T ) } , q _ { S t u d e n t ( T ) } )
Objective 1: Teach student to predict the true y
Objective 2: Teach student to behave like Teacher, who is good at predicting y but is too large
Knowledge Distillation
▶ What's the idea of Knowledge Distillation?
You want the student to still learn things from the teacher; here, 'learns' means same probabilities per target
You want the student answers to be similar to the teacher answers
Case 1: If the teacher is too sure ( p (1) j is high for one particular j ), T →∞ smooths outs the distribution so p ( T ) becomes uniform; this adds a bit of hedging
Case 2: If you want to just learn from the teacher directly, you can use T ∼ 0 and in the limit you use y t j = 1 if j = argmax ( p i ) otherwise y t j = 0
Alternative: for 'hard' Knowledge Distillation you can use

Distil-BERT
Can we get a much smaller model than BERT with performance that is on par?
The teacher model is BERT BASE , from the BERT paper
The authors of Distil-BERT tried to do just this
Their loss L had three terms
L ce = ∑ j p ( T ) j log q ( T ) j where p ( T ) i , q ( T ) i are the Teacher, Student probabilities

Specifically, for a new u , this is O (1) forward passes (get the embedding of u ) since we cached the embeddings of v and can just do O (log N ) approximate nearest neighbor search

Word Embeddings Motivation
Given : A corpus C with sentences
Want : A vector representation for each word a w such that the vector a w captures some of the 'meaning' of w for each w
Ideally : These vectors have nice mathematical properties
If two words w 1 , w 2 are related, their vectors are related in the sense that a ⊺ w 1 a w 2 is high
Or, || a w 1 -a w 2 || is low: we can cluster similar words!
Maybe a w 1 -a w 2 has a 'meaning'; a doctor -a man ∼ 'medical profession person'
Why Word Embeddings: Motivation
But Why? ... There are a few textbook answers:

Traditional approaches treated words as independent entities, ignoring their meanings and relationships. Word embeddings, however, represent words as dense vectors in a continuous space, capturing semantic similarities (e.g., 'king' and 'queen' are close in the embedding space)
One-hot encoding creates sparse, high-dimensional vectors (with a dimension for every unique word), which leads to inefficiencies in computation and storage
Pretrained embeddings can be used across various NLP applications, reducing the need for large labeled datasets
Tasks like sentiment analysis, machine translation, and named entity recognition benefit from embeddings, as they provide richer word representations than simple frequency-based methods
Models like Word2Vec utilize context to learn word meanings. This helps in better distinguishing between usage (e.g.,'river bank' vs. 'money bank')
For Word2Vec, we actually always have 1 embedding for a word, so averaging the two embeddings above is crucial
Word Embeddings: From Str to Int
Tokenize C in some way
Possible recipe: Take the tokens as the words
Take C and split it on spaces
Eliminate rare words?
Collapse some words together (running, run, runs)?
We now have a vocabulary set V with a set of words { a, and, the, cat, mat, ... }
Each word can be assigned a unique integer, and we have a hash map stoi = { w → i }
From this map, we can easily get a reverse hash map itos = { i → w }
For each w ∈ V we can assign a unique integer i so words can be represented as integers
Naively, we can represent each word as a one-hot 1-0 vector e in R |V| so that each element of e is 0 except the i th element which is 1
Thus, we have e [ stoi [ w ]] = 1 and e is zero for all other indices
Word Embeddings Motivation: One-Hot Example
Suppose C = 'The cat was blue.'
Suppose V = { 'the', 'cat', 'was', 'blue', '.' } so |V| = 5
Map each word to a unique index so that stoi = { 'the' → 0, 'cat' → 1, 'was' → 2, 'blue' → 3, '.' → 4 }
Then, a one-hot representation might be
Word Embeddings Motivation: One-Hot Problems
If V is a large set, the vectors we get for each word are very large
The notion of 'similarity' is lost, each vector is independent
Specifically, for any two different words i and j we have
a ⊺ wi a wj = 0
So, we want better representations and we have some goals
Goal 1 : Try to reduce the size of this space from R |V| to something smaller
Goal 2 : Hope that we can find a subspace that encodes the relationships between words so that
a ⊺ wi a wj = relationship ( a number )
SVD ?
Idea of SVD: express your data as a matrix and 'factorize' the matrix so that you capture the 'latent' dimensions of some subspace
Given some (data summary) matrix X , rewrite X = A Σ B ⊺
Can use the co-occourrence matrix X
What does it mean for something to co-occur? To in a certain window or distance from each other?
Creating X is not easy!
Word Embeddings Motivation: SVD
Fix a window size of 1
Given the 3 sentences below so S = { s 1 , s 2 , s 3 } , we can tokenize them into words and get a square matrix of dimension |V| × |V|
s 1 = 'I like driving.'
s 2 = 'I think NLP is cool .'
s 3 = 'Deep learning NLP is cool .'
V = { i , like , driving , ., think , nlp , is , cool , deep , learning }
Notice that '.' is a valid token
Word Embeddings Motivation: SVD
For any word, again set a unique word integer i via stoi [ w ] = i
For any two words w i and w j let X [ i , j ] = number of times w i and w j are within a window size 1 of each other
We get the symmetric matrix X :
