Word Embeddings Motivation: SVD
Perform SVD on X so that X = A Σ B ⊺
A , Σ, B is |V| × |V|
Σ is diagonal and has singular values σ i on it's diagonal
Pick some K < |V| so that ∑ K i =1 σ i ∑ |V| i =1 σ i is high enough
SVD Word Embeddings
Approximate X by ˆ X where we approximate
A by ˆ A , where we use only the first K columns
Σ by ˆ Σ where we only use the first K rows and K columns
B by ˆ B , where we use only the first K rows
We get an Approximation X ∼ ˆ X = ˆ A ˆ Σ ˆ B ⊺
Note that the |V| K -dimensional rows of ˆ A or/and ˆ B can be viewed as vector representations for each word
Each row of ˆ A gives us a K -dimensional vector a w
Each row of ˆ B gives us a K -dimensonal vector b w
We have a vector representation with dimension K < |V|
Maybe a w is what we want? Maybe these vectors have the nice properties we wanted?
SVD Has Problems
Some problems:
X is very sparse
Expensive cost to train: O ( |V| 3 )
New words imply that the dimension of the matrix changes very often
Very large matrix!
X usually is not just co-occurrence counts, we usually need more than this and some hacks to make this work best (so the rows of A capture as much semantic and syntactic information as possible)
Can use Pearson correlation instead of counts
Remove function words like 'he', 'has', 'the', 'a', etc.
Use a ramp window: weigh co-occurence matrix by distance instead of just counts
Instead of this, let's try something else!
Word Embeddings Motivation: Towards Word2Vec
Idea: Instead of computing global statistics, we create a model that learns at each iteration and refines what it learns as it progresses
The main paper (from 2013) below has the core ideas we look at next
Efficient Estimation of Word Representations in Vector Space
After this paper, there were a few generalization and followups but we will probably skip them (ask me if interested!)
These Methods: static word embeddings, where each word has 1 representation
For example, with s 1 ='On the river bank' and s 2 ='I robbed the bank' there is only 1 vector for 'bank'
BERT and Modern Embeddings: words don't have 1 embedding (in the above, 'bank' gets a different representation depending on its context)
Review: Softmax, Sigmoid, and Cross Entropy
Softmax : given a R |V| dimensional vector z , we want to turn z into a probability
This pre-probability is usually called a logit
We can map z → softmax ( z ) = ( e z 1 ∑ |V| j =1 e z j , . . . , e z |V| ∑ |V| j =1 e z j )
This preserves the order among the components, and is differentiable!
z → max ( z ) is NOT differentiable!
Sigmoid : given a real number, we can map it to a probability via z → σ ( z ) = 1 1+ e -z
This is used in Logistic Regression, and we will need it here as well
Review: Softmax, Sigmoid, and Cross Entropy
Cross-Entropy : Give two distributions q and p over V , how do we measure how close they are?
The Cross-Entropy can be used measure the dissimilarity
Cross -Entropy ( p , q ) = -|V| ∑ i =1 q i log p i
If q is fixed, what is the optimal p ? You'll get p = q
To see this, minimize
L ( p ) = -|V| ∑ i =1 q i log p i
subject to ∑ |V| i =1 p i = 1
Typically for us q is the y , a one-hot vector and p is the Softmax of some Logit that a model predicts (a vector)
Review: Cross Entropy and Softmax vs using Max

Generally, if our model is trying to predict 1 of |V| classes and we return a vector z of dimension |V| , we can entice the model to give the highest component value to z to the true class y j by considering the loss
L = Cross -Entropy ( p , y ) = -|V| ∑ i =1 y i log p i = -log p j
where p = softmax ( z )
Consider an alternative where we'd try
L = -1 { argmax { z } == j }
so we directly involve the max and entice z to put the highest value of z j so that we 'predict' the right class
The last loss is very direct, but much more numerically difficult to optimize ... What's the gradient? When is it nonzero?

Word2Vec At A High Level
A high level summary:
2 models: CBOW and Skip-Gram
CBOW: predict a center word from the words around it
Skip-Gram: predict words around a center word
2 tricks: Negative Sampling and Hierarchical Softmax
Both these methods try to solve for the issue that |V| is a very large and leads to slow optimization as we'll see
Both models are built on the idea: 'You shall know a word by the company it keeps' (Distributional Hypothesis - Harris 1954)
I.e. Words that are close together or used in the same way tend to mean the same thing
Word Embeddings: CBOW
The goal of CBOW is to predict the missing center word from the neighboring context words

Word Embeddings: CBOW
We need to model p ( w output | w context )
Given a sequence 'student studied NLP into the' we can let NLP be the output word and 'student, studied, into, the' be the context
Define two matrices B ∈ R |V|× d and A ∈ R |V|× d where d represents the embedding space
If we have the one-hot representation of a word, e w ∈ R |V| , then
B ⊺ e w = b w ∈ R d
can be viewed as dimensional vector representation of w
We also have
A ⊺ e w = a w ∈ R d
Word Embeddings: CBOW Algorithm
Given a set of documents C , break up C into sentences and tokenize C to get the vocabulary V
Fix a window size m so for each word we look m words to the right and left
Example: if s = 'This is a short sentence.' and m = 1 we get
[' this ' , ' is ' , ' a ']
[' is ' , ' a ' , ' short ']
[' a ' , ' short ' , ' sentence ']
[' short ' , ' sentence ' , ' . ']
Word Embeddings: CBOW Algorithm
For each center out word w o and context words, gather ( x , y ) pairs like
[' a ' , ' short ' , ' sentence '] becomes [(' a ' , ' sentence ') , ' short '] and w o = ' short '
For each context word, get the one-hot representations e w ∈ R |V|
For each context word, get the vector representations ( A ⊺ e wo -m , A ⊺ e wo -1 , A ⊺ e wo +1 , . . . , A ⊺ e wo + m ) so that
A ⊺ e w = a w ∈ R d
etc.
Word Embeddings: CBOW Algorithm
Average the vectors a avg = aw o -m + ... + aw o + m 2 m ∈ R d
Generate logit scores z = Ba avg ∈ R |V|
Turn the scores into probabilities p = softmax( z ) ∈ R |V|
We hope that these probabilities p match the true probabilities y and we have the one-hot representation of the output word e wo = y
I.e. We hope that p wo ∼ 1 and p is zero elsewhere
Word Embeddings: CBOW Algorithm
Given that we want p ∼ y , we can focus on minimizing the loss (Cross-Entropy)
L ( A , B ) = -|V| ∑ i =1 e i log p i
Note that, since e is the one-hot vector of w o ; the above is actually just one term of the type -log p j where j is the index of w o in V ; stoi [ w o ] = j
If p j ∼ 1, as it should be, we get a 0 penalty
If p j ∼ 0, we get a + ∞ penalty!
If p j ∼ 1 10 , we get a loss of -log 1 10 = 4 . 605
In either of the last two cases, we've given too low a probability to something that should have a high probability so the loss will be large
Word Embeddings: CBOW Algorithm
The loss of a general output word w o and fixed window around it is:
L ( A , B ) = -log p ( w o | w o -m , . . . , w o -1 , w o +1 , . . . , w o + m ) = -log p ( w o | a avg ) = -log exp b ⊺ wo a avg ∑ w ∈V exp b ⊺ w a avg = -b ⊺ wo a avg +log ∑ w ∈V exp b ⊺ w a avg
Generally, the total loss of this model is the loss across the entire training data by windowing in this way and averaging over the number of windows
The parameters of this model are A and B
We can use SGD to update the parameters of this model
A new ← A old -η ∇ A L ( A old , B old )
B new ← B old -η ∇ B L ( A old , B old )
Word Embeddings: CBOW Gradients
The loss of a general model looks like an average of Cross -Entropy , where we divide by the number of windows (we'll see how to get the training data when we look at code)

As with CBOW, you have parameter embedding matrix A , B as before

Word Embeddings: Skip-Gram Model
Again: given a set of sentences C , tokenize at the word level and get the vocabulary V and fix a window size m
Get, from all sentences, all center (context) input words c and output words o pairs, gathering pairs like
( w c , w c -m ) , . . . , ( w c , w c + m )
so that output words are within m distance of the context word w c (on either side)
Note that in this case the center word is a feature ( x ) and the output word is the target ( y )
For each context center word, get the one-hot representations e wc ∈ R |V|
For each context center word, get the vector representations
a wc = A ⊺ e wc ∈ R d
Word Embeddings: Skip-Gram Model
Generate a score z = Ba wc ∈ R |V|
Turn the scores into probabilities p = softmax( z ) ∈ R |V|
We have that p wc -m , p wc -m +1 , . . . , p wc + m -1 , p wc + m , are the probabilities of observing the context words given the center word w c
As with CBOW, we want the probabilities above to match the one-hot vectors e wc -m , e wc -m +1 , . . . , e wc + m -1 , e wc + m
Specifically, for a fixed center word w c we have loss terms equal to
L ( A , B ) = -m ∑ i = -m , i =0 e wc + i log p wc + i
Word Embeddings: Skip-Gram Model
We can spell-out the above more by noticing that in our model we want to minimize:

L ( A , B ) = -log p ( w c -m , w c -m +1 , . . . , w c + m -1 , w c + m | w c ) = -log m ∏ i = -m , i =0 p ( w c + i | w c ) = -log m ∏ i = -m , i =0 exp b ⊺ wc + i a wc ∑ |V| j =1 exp b ⊺ wj a wc = -m ∑ i = -m , i =0 b ⊺ wc + i a wc +2 m log |V| ∑ j =1 exp b ⊺ wj a wc
Word Embeddings: Skip-Gram Model
Note that in the above, we've used the Naive Bayes' assumption p ( A , B | C ) = p ( A | C ) p ( B | C ) repeatedly
Note that in actuality we have that
L ( A , B ) = -m ∑ i = -m , i =0 log p ( b wc + i | a wc )
is the sum of 2 m terms
m ∑ i = -m , i =0 Cross -Entropy ( e c + i , softmax ( Ba wc ))
Skip-Gram Gradients
So, a general term of the Skip-Gram objective looks like
L ( A , B ) = -log p ( b wo | a wc )) = -b ⊺ wo a wc +log ∑ w ∈V exp b ⊺ w a wc
We have (HW!)
▶ ∂ L ∂ bwo = -a wc + awc exp b ⊺ wo awc ∑ u ∈V exp b ⊺ u awc ▶ ∂ L ∂ awc = -b wo + ∑ w ∈V b w exp b ⊺ w aw c ∑ u ∈V exp b ⊺ u awc
Notice that this is E [ b w ] -b wo where the expectation is using p ( w ) = exp b ⊺ w aw c ∑ u ∈V exp b ⊺ u awc
Gradient = Expected Guess - Hard Guess
Skip-Gram and CBOW issues
One big problem with the Skip-Gram and the CBOW model is the (normalizing) summation ∑ w ∈V exp b ⊺ w a wc
This term is the Softmax denominator so if we want to use the Softmax as is we need it
Second, because we use the Softmax, this term shows up in the gradient computations
Overall, computing a gradient is then a O ( |V| ) computation, which is expensive
Idea: Can we do better via some approximation method?
Softmax Meaning
Let z ∈ R |V| be some vector and p = softmax ( z )
Fix some j and consider p j when we have y j = 1 and y i = 0 if i = j
What does p j mean?
It means j is the true class, a positive sample
Also, from the denominator, we know that there are |V| 1 other classes, and they are negative with respect to p j
Using p j = e z j e z 1 + ... + e z |V| , a Softmax-based objective basically tries to make the logit z j as big as possible relative to the other logits z 1 , z 2 , z j -1 . . . z j +1 , z |V|
For us, the logit we want to push up is b ⊺ wo a wc since w c predicts w o
Also, want to push down b ⊺ a for any k = o
z = Ba ∈ R |V| here ...
wc
wk wc
Negative Sampling
So, consider the Skip-Gram model with center word w c and some context word w o ; we know that p ( w o | w c ) should be nonzero and we want it to be high
Thus, in this setting, we have that ( w c , w o ) is a positive (+1) sample, so we can use the sigmoid to approximate
p ( w o | w c ) ∼ σ ( b ⊺ wo a wc ) = 1 1 + exp -b ⊺ wo a wc
Similarly, we sample K words w k that are not in the context and we know these have a negative label (-1), so
p ( w k | w c ) ∼ 1 -σ ( b ⊺ wk a wc ) = σ ( -b ⊺ wk a wc ) = 1 1 + exp b ⊺ wk a wc
Negative Sampling
In particular, we replace
p ( w o | w c ) = exp b ⊺ wo a wc ∑ |V| j =1 exp b ⊺ wj a wc
by
p ( w o | w c ) = ( 1 1 + exp -b ⊺ wo a wc ) E wk ∼ psample ( w ) [ K ∏ k =1 1 1 + exp b ⊺ wk a wc ]
We sample each word w k from the empirical distribution of the words in the corpus
With high probability, for a given w c , these words will not be in the context of w c
Word Embeddings: Negative Sampling
With this approximation, we replace the original term in the objective
-log p ( w o | w c ) = -b ⊺ wo a wc +log |V| ∑ j =1 exp b ⊺ wj a wc

Word Embeddings: Negative Sampling + Dropout
Sampling the K negative samples is an art
The authors of Skip-Gram used p sample ( w ) ∝ freq ( w ) 3 4
The 3/4 has the effect of pushing less frequent words up more than frequent words
Before doing anything through, the authors also drop words from the corpus
p keep ( w ) = ( √ z ( w ) 0 . 001 +1) 0 . 001 z ( w ) where z ( w ) ∝ freq ( w ) in the original corpus

Why does this work?
Let's focus on CBOW and use a window of size 3
In this case, we have ( u , v , w ) as 3 words and we use the average A representations of u and w to modify v 's B representation so the inner product we want is large
( a u + a w ) ⊺ b v 2
So I have a sentence like 'the queen ruled' and another sentence like 'the king reigned'
Given this, we have that the A vectors of ( the , ruled ) (averaged) is enticed to be like the B vector of queen
We also have that the A vectors of ( the , reigned ) (averaged) is enticed to be like the B vector of king
We do this many times, and get that the B vectors of 'queen' and 'king' are both enticed to be similar, since they are similar to the same words
Doing this over and over, we have that for any word it's ( a + b ) / 2 vector is similar to all semantically similar words
Hierarchical Softmax : Another trick to bypass O ( |V| )
Hierarchical Softmax is an efficient way of computing Softmax. The model uses a binary tree to represent all words in the vocabulary. The V words must be leaf units of the tree
For each leaf unit, there exists a unique path from the root to the unit; and this path is used to estimate the probability of the word represented by the leaf unit
Main advantage: we replace the O ( |V| ) Softmax cost with O (log |V| )
The speed of this method depends on how the binary tree is built and words are associated with the leaf nodes; the paper uses a binary Huffman tree
Good project? This layer is not available in PyTorch by default
